responseSet.title: Clinical Approach to a Narcissistic Aspect
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Approach to a Narcissistic Aspect

Roberto Guerrini

Roberto Guerrini

Roberto A. Guerrini
Damián Hudson 438
5500 Mendoza
Argentina

Roberto Guerrini  

I should like first of all to welcome the IJPA/IRPA Editorial Board's excellent idea of illustrating through these presentations how psychoanalysts in different parts of the world work day after day and session after session with patients.

I begin my paper by introducing the case, giving the reason for consultation and some personal information about the patient; I also add some information gleaned during the course of the analysis, which helps to convey the climate of the sessions, but omit other points on grounds of discretion. I then transcribe two sessions from consecutive days, Tuesday and Wednesday.

HISTORY

When the 43-year-old female patient came for consultation, she had had an analysis lasting several years and wanted to continue with a view to solving the problem of her frigidity, which she presented as the sole reason for consultation. Once this had been said, I felt that I had lost the patient, as her attitude was rather as if there was nothing else to talk about and it merely remained for me to set the days, times and fees.

My countertransference feeling was of the kind of patient who comes to a medical consultation, explains the symptom and expects in response the immediate supply of the drug that will solve the problem. I could not discern any emotional commitment whatsoever in what she was requesting or looking for, and I therefore considered that the isolated aspect of frigidity brought by the patient would assume a more serious dimension as a personality disorder in the transference-countertransference relationship.

At first I could presume that what she called frigidity was a devitalization of the link, or a non-link. I began to ask questions: she had been married for 15 years and had no children; the reasons she gave were very confused; sometimes she said it was because her husband had told her he was not prepared to have any, while at others it was she who did not want them because she was afraid of not knowing how to treat a baby. At yet other times, she said it was because of an operation she had had, but the details are still not clear to me today, because I think they are not clear to her either (a haemorrhage through a Graafian follicle which had perforated an artery, followed by a second operation for section of a tube and removal of adhesions from the other tube). Whenever she talked about this in the interviews and later during the analysis, the climate was one of confusion and imprecision, the countertransference taking the form of a powerful experience of something sinister—it seems that the husband had wanted and tried to have her sterilized and had asked the surgeon to do this.

responseSet.title: Bruno Bettelheim (1903-1990)
responseSet.authorMast: Bertram J. Cohler and  Jacqueline Sanders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bruno Bettelheim (1903-1990)

Bertram Cohler and Jacqueline Sanders

Jacqueline Sanders

Bertram J. Cohler
Jacqueline Sanders
Committee on Human Development
5730 South Woodlawn Avenue
Chicago
Illinois 60637

Jacqueline Sanders  

Bruno Bettelheim's death at age 86, marked by his apparent feeling of desperation at the end of an unusually productive life, is a particularly sad occasion for his family, friends, and colleagues. For nearly thirty years, Bettelheim was director of the Sonia Shankman Orthogenic School of the University of Chicago, and part of a generation which also included Peter Blos, Sr., Rudolph Ekstein, Erik Erikson, Willi Hoffer, Fritz Redl, Siegfried Bernfeld and others, educated primarily in Vienna, who pioneered in the application of psychoanalysis to the study, education, and treatment of children and adolescents under the guidance of Anna Freud. In the case of Bettelheim and Redl, collaboration in the late nineteen-forties led to the concept of the therapeutic milieu in which psychoanalytic concepts were applied in the construction of an environment in which every aspect of the physical and social surround was informed by psychoanalytic concepts in order that maximum effort might be brought to bear on the child's treatment.

Bettelheim was born in Vienna to an upper middle-class Jewish family and spent his adolescence and early adulthood in a time characterized by exciting intellectual contributions to literature and the arts. Fascinated by Freud's writings, analysed by Richard Sterba in Vienna, Bettelheim saw psychoanalysis as the most vital of a series of intellectual movements promising renewed appreciation of the complexity and dignity of the human condition. Completing doctoral studies in philosophy and art history, his intellectual interests were fostered by the exciting intellectual climate of his youth. A businessman and social activist of the left, Bettelheim became involved in political and social movements which, ultimately, brought him into conflict with National Socialism, and his incarceration in Buchenwald during 1939.

Immigrating to the United States, Bettelheim began by teaching psychology and art history at Rockford College from 1942–1944, when he was appointed to the faculty of The University of Chicago. Bettelheim's initial work at Chicago was in the area of art and education; his interest in psychoanalysis and education led to his appointment as director of the Orthogenic School and, ultimately, he became the Stella M. Rowley Distinguished Service Professor, the highest honour conferred by the University upon one of its faculty. Married to Trude Weinfeld shortly after coming to the United States, the Bettelheims and their three children, Ruth, Naomi, and Eric became important members of the Hyde Park community near the university.

International Psychoanalytical Association Standing Conference on Psychoanalytic Research London, 19–21 March 1992

The Standing Conference aims at fostering the mutual influences of psychoanalytical research and clinical work, and is designed to appeal to both clinicians and research workers. The Programme Committee for this meeting will be headed by Robert S. Wallerstein. The language of the Conference will be English, and the number of places will be limited. The Conference will commence with registration and a reception on the evening of Thursday, March 19 and will end on Saturday, March 21. Details and registration forms will be available from The Conference Secretary, The Psychoanalysis Unit, University College London, 26 Bedford Way, London WC1H 0AP. The registration fee will be £75 until January 15, 1992 and £95 thereafter.

Pre-Oedipal Reconstructions in Psychoanalysis

Arthur Valenstein M.D.

Arthur Valenstein

Arthur F. Valenstein, M.D.
140A Foster Street
Cambridge
Massachusetts 02138

Arthur Valenstein  

Dear Sir:

It has been brought to my attention by Dr. Jonathan Cohen that in my 1989 article, 'Preoedipal reconstructions in psychoanalysis' (Int. J. Psychoanal., 70: 433–442), in quoting myself (p. 440) from an earlier 1973 article 'On attachment to painful feelings and the negative therapeutic reaction' (Psychoanal. Study Child, 28: 363–392), I inadvertently departed from the original text (p. 390), as follows:

psychoanalytic interpretations (in the usual sense) … cannot really reach the preverbal-earliest verbal level of development … However, if the therapist persists in working towards the possibility that the patient can and will progressively and adaptively use both what is recapitulated and articulately remembered and what is behaviorally recapitulated and reconstructed from the time when it cannot be literally remembered, then the outcome might be the achievement of a significant measure of corrective experiential re-education supplemented by cognitive understanding, i.e. mutative insight.

The quotation should actually have read:

psychoanalytic interpretations … cannot really reach the preverbal-earliest verbal level of development … However, if the therapist persists in working toward the possibility that the patient can and will progressively and adaptively use both what is recapitulated and articulately remembered, and what is behaviorally recapitulated and reconstructed from the time when it cannot be literally remembered, then the outcome might be the achievement of a significant measure of corrective experiential re-education supplemented by cognitive understanding.

December 1990

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Forces of Destiny: Psychoanalysis and Human Idiom

By Christopher Bollas. London: Free Association Books. 1989. Pp. 223.

John Padel

John Padel

John Padel
Hinton Waldrist, Oxon SN7 8RN

John Padel  

This book's title declares the author's love of words and phrases. The main title (nothing to do with Verdi) may seem grandiloquent, until it becomes apparent that Bollas is already urging 'the analyst's celebration of the analysand' (the title of Chapter 4) and that 'destiny', as opposed to 'fate', is chosen to include the notions of unconscious intention and striving, which apply to the least of us and not only to heroic figures such as Aeneas and Napoleon. The subtitle's 'idiom' has here the meaning, justifiable etymologically, of 'that which is uniquely personal'.

Bollas needs these extensions of meaning to re-think psychoanalytic theory in his own original way. Indeed his originality is shown in his clinical approach as well as in his formulations of theory. A difficult case or a piece of infuriating behaviour, successfully or unsuccessfully treated, will lead him to new thought upon theory and the insight he gains is at once turned back upon other cases.

The book is in two parts. The first five chapters have several new concepts, new as technical terms—'destiny drive', 'futures', 'topographic return', 'idiom-moves'—besides some which Bollas proposed in his previous book—'the transformational object', 'the unthought known'. So in the Introduction he suggests that these chapters be read in sequence, where the concepts are spelled out as they first occur.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1991

[[RunningHead]]

Masochism. The Treatment of Self-Inflicted Suffering.

Edited by Jill D. Montgomery and Ann C. Greif. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1989. pp. 239.

Franklin Maleson

Franklin Maleson

Franklin G. Maleson
1015 Chestnut Street, Philadelphia, PA 19107

Franklin Maleson  

This collection of papers was written by a group of colleagues working on a long-term basis with severely disturbed inpatients at the Austin Riggs Center in Massachusetts. Some of these patients—deemed masochistic by the authors—presented such difficulties in management and understanding that they became the subject of ongoing study groups and symposia, and ultimately this book. Treatments were highly turbulent, characterized by much acting out, milieu turmoil, therapeutic impasse and crisis, self-destructiveness or provocation of attacks, and intense transference-countertransference reactions. These difficulties, coupled with the perception that the analytic literature did not adequately address them, stimulated the publication of these papers. 'We began to develop a growing dissatisfaction with the standard interpretation of these [masochistic] patients and their behaviors … we found formulations that typically stressed the aggressive component of this behavior, or instinctual gratification and superego retribution, as the primary level of interpretation to be useless if not actually destructive' (p. xiii, my italics).

The statements just quoted illustrate one of the many weaknesses of this book. In dismissing volumes of prior thought on masochism by analytic scholars, the authors (especially the editors) display a tendency to make overly bold and sweeping generalizations and to argue against straw men. For example, currently there is no 'standard interpretation' of masochism. In many, perhaps most modern writings, so-called masochistic phenomena are viewed as quite heterogeneous in origin and nature, highly overdetermined and serving multiple psychic functions (e. g. Blum, 1976); (Maleson, 1984); (Grossman, 1986); (Simons, 1987). There is no single 'masochism'. To further illustrate the straw man tendency, it is stated that 'Traditionally, the literature has assumed the superior capacity of the analyst to formulate insights and to lend these to the patient … the more or less passive recipient of the decisive action taken by the analyst' (p. 12). The freeing of the patient's own interpretive initiative is treated as a new idea. (This quality of fresh discovery of the already familiar is commonplace in this book.)

[[RunningHead]]

Thomas Forrest Main (1911–1990)

T. Hayley

T. Hayley

T. T.S. Hayley
5 Upper Wimpole Street
London W1M 7TD

T. Hayley  

Tom Main, who died on 29 May 1990, was best known world-wide as the creator of the therapeutic community at the Cassel Hospital rather than as a psychoanalyst. No paper of his appeared in the International Journal or Review of Psycho-Analysis. Yet the many facets of his life's work came under the strong influence of his psychoanalytical knowledge and convictions and up to his death he treated a number of patients in his private psychoanalytical practice.1

He was born on 25 February 1911 in Johannesburg. His father, a ship's carpenter, had emigrated there from Tyneside and had become a mine manager by the time Tom was born. He had an elder sister and another three years younger. In 1914 the three children returned with their mother to Tyneside, while Tom's father joined the South African army at the outbreak of the First World War. The prosperity they had enjoyed in South Africa disappeared and the family lived in some poverty, Tom being boarded out with aunts some of the time. After the war his father, having been disabled in the war, returned to Tyneside and another son was born, who died tragically of pneumonia when 14 years old.

Tom won a scholarship to the Royal Grammar School, Newcastle-upon-Tyne. He was taught by Michael Roberts, a recognized poet of the time. Tom, opines his friend, Colin Legum, would have made an ideal Poet Laureate. He used to write poems to mark the birthdays of his many children, grandchildren, and godchildren. However, all the school prizes he won were taken in the form of books of music, Tom being devoted to classical music. He was an outstanding Rugby football player. At the age of 16 he won a scholarship to the Medical College of the University of Durham, where he met his future wife, Agnes Mary (Molly) McHaffie. They married on 27 February 1937. Tom graduated with Honours in 1932, obtaining his M.D. in 1934. He was greatly influenced by the paediatrician, James Spence, and he had read The Interpretation of Dreams when he was still at school. This decided him to specialise in psychiatry. After a period at Rainhill he obtained a consultant post at Gateshead Mental Hospital in Northumberland.

[[RunningHead]]

The European Federation of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

A European Federation of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy (EFPP) for EC and EFTA countries was founded on 8 February 1991.

Fifty delegates from the majority of the 18 countries attended a three-day meeting in London, which concluded with the adoption of a Constitution and the first meeting of its elected Executive Committee.

The Federation is to focus on public health and related services where third parties fund treatment. Its main aims are to benefit the public by promoting the development of both psychoanalytic psychotherapy services and the training of therapists to a high standard and also to promote further research. The Federation will want to bring to notice the scarcity of treatment resources in many of the countries.

The officers of the Executive Committee are:

Chair: Dr Brian Martindale (London)

Vice-Chair: Dr John Tsiantis (Greece)

Coordinators:

Adult section Dr Serge Frisch (Luxembourg)

Group section Dr Göran Ahlin (Sweden)

Children & Adolescents Ms Birgit Hallerfors (Sweden)

Secretary Mrs Lydia Tischler (London)

A. Watillon

A. Watillon
90, Avenue Armand Huysmans
1050 Bruxelles

A. Watillon  

On the basis of a clinical example, I should like to illustrate the thesis that acting out by the analyst in the countertransference is a response to the patient's material and, if its unconscious meaning is worked on and elaborated by the analyst and the analysand, can lead to a better understanding of the latter's problems (and also those of the analyst, but that remains a non-verbalized gain). Countertransference reactions fall into a context of interaction between patient and analyst which constitutes a therapeutic lever (Langs, 1988).

I certainly have no wish to deny or even minimize the fact that acting out in the countertransference also has to do with the unresolved, personal unconscious conflicts of the analyst, or that it will necessarily be traumatic for the patient.

Although the analyst will obviously do his best to prevent such irruptions of his unconscious into the session, where a mistake of this kind has nevertheless occurred, it will, if fully acknowledged by him, lead to a thoroughgoing process of transformation and psychic elaboration.

The acting out which I shall describe to you consisted in an error in evaluating the time of the session: I indicated to my patient that the session was over a quarter of an hour early.

Before introducing Sophie to you, I should like to thank her for everything she has taught me and allowed me to understand about her, myself and mental mechanisms. I should also like to express my regret for the suffering I caused her by my mistake and to apologize for the inevitable distortions involved in the writing up of a clinical case history. It is impossible truly to communicate all the exchanges that took place in the patient–analyst interaction and to convey the emotional shifts and affective density of the transference and countertransference movements occurring in an analytical session. I shall endeavour to stay as close as possible to the truth and to be as honest as I can.

HISTORY

Sophie was in her twenties when we first met. She was unmarried and had a successful professional career. She had already had other analytical treatments, which, she said, had helped her to manage in her everyday life, but felt that she was now going round in circles. She could not form a stable relationship with a man and considered that she was constantly repeating the same patterns of behaviour in her relations with men.

The Babel of the Unconscious

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua
Corazón de María 2
28002 Madrid

Cecilio Paniagua  

Dear Sir:

I would like to congratulate Drs Amati Mehler, Argentieri and Canestri for their article 'The babel of the unconscious' (I.J.P.A., 70: 569, 1990). Polyglotism is an important issue in the history and present practice of psychoanalysis, and there have been few works indeed addressing its significance and technical management.

I had a personal analysis in my mother-tongue and another one in a different language. I have analysed patients in a language other than my mother-tongue. I have analysed patients who preferred using two languages in the same session. I analysed one who, although sharing mother-tongue with me, chose to speak a second language for the first two years of his treatment. These experiences are fairly common, especially in Europe (see, for instance, Flegenheimer's recent article 'Languages and psychoanalysis …', I.R.P.A., 16: 377, 1989).

Amati Mehler et al. wondered 'How can the condition of multilingualism become a vehicle that enhances structuring defences or pathology?' (p. 572). In my experience, speaking a language other than the mother-tongue in analysis gets used unfailingly at the service of isolation of affect. It is also very frequently the fantasy that through the second language ideal aspects of the self get conveyed. Significant narcissistic pathology may pass unnoticed when the analysis is conducted in a language other than the mother-tongue if the possibility of the latter phenomenon is not taken into consideration.

Amati Mehler et al. mention that some analysts 'insist' that the analysand uses the mother-tongue; it is supposed to be 'a desirable technical expedient' (p. 575). 'Insistence' seems to me an unnecessary suggestive manoeuvre. As a result of the analysis of the resistance against using the mother-tongue (the language of 'thing representations') sooner or later an analysable patient will become aware of protective utilization of his isolation of affect and the narcissistic gratification inherent in the illusory use of the second language.

The Ontogeny of Silence in an Analytic Case

Janet Hadda

Janet Hadda

Janet Hadda
850 S. Masselin Avenue
Los Angeles
CA 90036

Janet Hadda  

SUMMARY

This paper concerns the uses of silence by an analytic patient, with particular emphasis on silence as a changing means of communication. The patient's initial use of silence was a way of indicating to me her original relationship with her mother; it also represented an unstated appeal that I grant her a measure of autonomy.

Later, silence signalled several stages along the way to the patient's full articulation of her longing to be welcomed, to be enthusiastically enjoyed by me. My role in these later manifestations of silence was that of aiding her in the articulation of hopes and wishes, stifled since early childhood because of an unfortunate series of abandonments and experiences of humiliation. The culmination of this articulation process was the patient's ability to vary a repetitive dream that she had had before the start of her analysis.

The Case of a Young University Student

Mauro Morra

Mauro Morra

Mauro Morra
Via L. Biasioli, 321
16167 Genova
Italy

Mauro Morra  

I am happy to respond to the invitation for clinical accounts, which came out in the Editorial of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis. Indeed I feel that the search for Common Ground in psychoanalysis is much easier if we deal with practical day-to-day work than if we want to compare theoretical issues.

I would like to present the case of a young man, whom I call Gennaro, who was 18 years old when he started his analysis with me. He was born in a small town of Southern Italy, where his family still resides. He lived there until he moved to the town where I live and work, in Northern Italy, Genova.

The reasons for his move were both his wish to attend university and his determination to be analysed (he had previously discussed his difficulties with another psychoanalyst whom he had met socially).

He came to me with his parents. First I interviewed him by himself, and then, considering his youth and his still being financially dependent on his parents, I decided to have another meeting with him and his parents together. In the first interview he mainly complained of some occasional moments of diminution of sight (when he was moved emotionally) together with some vague and painful feelings of getting lost (depersonalization?). In addition to this, he suffered from agoraphobia, and also from difficulty in concentrating on his school work. He mentioned that he was rather isolated from his friends, but tended to minimize this problem. He had a girl-friend (considered his fiancée) in his home town, but he had a lot of difficulties with her as well.

HISTORY

His history was the following: he knew that his mother had become pregnant unmarried, and for this reason his father had married her when both were very young (in their early twenties). His father's parents were not very happy with such a marriage mainly for two reasons: first the girl was judged as bad from a moral point of view, second she was inferior with regard to social status. Just a few months after Gennaro's birth, his mother became pregnant for the second time and, when another boy was born, she realized that she was not able to cope with two young children. So Gennaro was taken by his paternal grandparents and lived with them apparently happily; they were resident out of the town in a nice little cottage with a garden. Anyway he kept some contact with his parents and brother, and also a little sister who was born four years after him. The relationship between the two families was rather cold, and grandmother used to criticize her daughter-in-law openly in the presence of Gennaro.

[[RunningHead]]

Developmental Antecedents of Masochism: Vignettes from the Analysis of a 3-Year-Old Girl

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman
4180 La Jolla Village Drive, Suite 550B
La Jolla
CA 92037

Alan Sugarman  

SUMMARY

This paper suggests that masochism be understood as an organizing concept which involves drives, defence mechanisms, superego elements, self and object representations as well as environmentally based interpersonal relationships. Vignettes from the analysis of a 3-year-old girl who presented with protomasochistic provocativeness are offered to illustrate the multiple functions served by such behaviour. The opportunity to study such developmental antecedents of masochism demonstrates that such symptoms have intrapsychic meanings even at such an early age. The role of external reality must be considered in regard to its structuring and stimulating influences on intrapsychic factors rather than regarded as the sole cause of masochism. Finally, it is important to realize that masochism has forerunners at all developmental stages rather than to try to reduce its origins to any one developmental epoch.

Dean Eyre  

Ms A presented some three years ago for psychoanalysis having been referred by a colleague. A somewhat overweight woman, she referred to herself as 'fat' having put on forty-five pounds, she said, in the last five years. The history she gave at this time, which over the course of the next three years she corrected, was that she was born and grew up in America. Her father was a very severe alcoholic with a lot of very violent behaviour and abusive, sexual acting out towards the other children in the family, particularly her elder sister who had died in a mental institution, as well as a younger sister. There was also a strong history of epilepsy in the family, an elder sister having died of epilepsy and another child, a brother who was handicapped and being looked after by her mother, who has now moved to the southern United States. She says she has very little contact with her mother now and virtually none with her brother and sister, who are still living.

She could not give very much history of her childhood apart from the violence and the fact that she lived in a poor area of town with Puerto Ricans and blacks. She was not allowed to play with them though she saw them at school all the time. She said her father also had a longstanding affair with another woman, and, as her mother worked every night on night duty as a nurse, this woman was frequently around the home. Father controlled the children from seeing anything by forbidding them to come out of their rooms at night. After high school she left America and went to western Canada and took a degree where she met her husband who was in a PhD. programme. They married, and when he had finished his degree they went to his country of origin for a period of three or four years. However, when she wanted to return to America he did not want to come back. She came back with a son, who is now 15. Her husband came to Canada for a short period of time but they felt they could not be reconciled with their fundamental differences and attitudes, so they finally separated and divorced. She obtained a job in the city where she took her degree and was there for a number of years. She had, as she termed it, a weekend of therapy, an encounter group, which greatly frightened her and upset her. She has now been in Ottawa for the last year. One of the reasons, she said, was to come back to something more familiar, like the eastern part of continental America.

[[RunningHead]]

Four Therapeutic Approaches to the Borderline Patient: Principles and Techniques of the Basic Dynamic Stances

By Andrew Druck. Northvale, New Jersey and London: Jason Aronson. 1989. Pp. 440.

Arthur Farley

Arthur Farley

Arthur J. Farley
6300 West Loop South, Suite 140, Bellaire, Texas 77401

Arthur Farley  

If one takes for granted that the 'borderline' patient exists, then what are the basic dynamic stances from which to attempt to understand and treat him? Andrew Druck lists four: (1) Kernberg's model (2) the representational-deficit/self-deficit models (3) the ego-deficit model and (4) the modifications of classical technique model.

It was a curious process to review a book about 'the borderline patient' because there has been so much material produced on the subject in the recent past. Was this going to be another 'borderline' book which would be an incomprehensible rehash of the many psychoanalytic contributions on the subject? Would it be idiosyncratic and lack a comprehensive perspective or simply 'grind another axe'? Would it conclude that 'borderline patients' are unanalysable because of their diagnosis?

Basically, I found that it cut through the borderline maze adeptly. Furthermore, I found it useful. It clearly states that psychoanalytic psychotherapy with the 'borderline patient' is more difficult than with others and why this group of patients is difficult. The author's main thesis asserts the close connexion between theory and technique in each therapist/patient interaction, i.e. that theoretical assumptions are important determinants of therapists' responses to patients. The therapist's awareness of these assumptions enables consistency with the patient and broadens his treatment options. Druck also emphasizes that the ultimate and most important determinant of the success of an 'analytic treatment' is the therapist's character, especially with those patients who are severely disturbed.

Therapists differ in their capacity to allow for regressive states, in their capacity to hold the patient through such states without major modifications in the treatment situation, and in their capacity to respond therapeutically to these states (p. 396).

I would recommend that each reader of Four Therapeutic Approaches to the Borderline Patient approach this work from his own particular perspective to see where he fits or does not fit. Readers will have various and multiple perspectives, e.g. level of and types of training, quantity and quality of experience and theoretical bents, just as each professional approaches a given patient. Druck points out the importance of the fit in this relationship. The eloquence of this work is its attention to the whys and the wherefores of the psychotherapist/patient fit.

1425 Buenos Aires

Jorge Ahumada  

SUMMARY

Discrimination of analogic and digital modes in communication allows clearer distinction of transference (mutative) and extra-transference interpretation. Relying on explicit formulation, Strachey introduces 'implicit' mutative interpretation, which is explicitly extra-transferential but transferential as to process.

Each interpretive verbalization has an analogic impact pertaining to the analogic unconscious level of archaic objects, and its emitter—the analyst—is its propositional subject. Interpretation can afford to be as explicit as possible inasmuch as it retains the metalevel in which the analyst can remain as third-party to what he formulates, but it must be gradual in making explicit the archaic object he actualizes.

It is by 'withholding'—as Strachey says—the validation of the archaic relational analogic 'psychic reality' that is the referent of interpretive 'mapping', and not by verbal 'mapping' in itself, that the 'judgement of reality' underlying ostensive insight comes into play.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Equilibrium and Psychic Change: Selected Papers of Betty Joseph

Edited by Elizabeth Bott Spillius and Michael Feldman. London and New York: Tavistock/Routledge. 1989. (New Library of Psychoanalysis, No. 9, published in association with the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London.) Pp. 230.

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer
241 Central Park West, Ste. 1A, New York, NY 10024

Roy Schafer  

This volume, comprising a remarkably illuminating series of essays, spans thirty years in the career of a leading figure in the evolution of modern Kleinian thought. Miss Joseph's odyssey as a Kleinian analyst is itself a fascinating study, most helpfully narrated herein by her dedicated colleagues and editors in their introductory and transitional commentaries. I shall not retrace their path through this history as to do so would be only to repeat their concise account. Mrs Spillius has also edited the two-volume work, Melanie Klein Today, in an equally informative way.

That there has evolved a modern Kleinian approach may come as a surprise to those anchored well within the confines of Freudian ego psychology. For it is hard to think of each differing school of psychoanalysis as having its own history, that is, as having developed beyond the thoughts of the school's founder and his or her circle. We do not ordinarily attribute process, diversity, or controversy to these schools, tending rather to limit ourselves to some first-hand or second-hand perusal of the founders' classical works. As outsiders, we either look for or simply assume the presence of homogeneity and constancy over time, hoping thereby to establish a clear, recognizable and safe picture of what initially is strange to us. The result of this simplification can only be excessive reliance on selective citation and other stereotyping devices rather than an entry into the complexities of continuing dialogue; in the end one is left with a handful of alienating clichés of one's own or one's teachers.

How necessary then is the appearance of this virtually complete collection of Miss Joseph's (mostly) published and (some) unpublished papers. They provide a broad and helpful Kleinian survey of familiar concepts, clinical phenomena, and difficult technical problems. There are discussions of sado-masochism in relationships; the apparently unreachable, un-understandable, or emotionally dead patient (a particular interest of the author); psychic pain; and the operation of projective identification in the transference. Additionally, there are particularly instructive discussions of perversion, primitive anxiety and its regulation by defence throughout psychic development, termination and numerous other aspects of the psychoanalytic process. And throughout these explorations we find a multitude of clinical illustrations that include fine examples of reconstructive interpretation, dream analysis, the weighing of alternative interventions (one wishes for still more of that) and the consistent application of countertransference analysis to the clinical process.

[[RunningHead]]

A Perversion Named Desire

Enrique Torres

Enrique Torres

Enrique R. Torres
Pje. Valtodano 1455
5000 Cordoba
Argentina

Enrique Torres  

SUMMARY

Inspired by the title of the work, A Streetcar Named Desire by Tennessee Williams, the author argues that desire and perversion are equivalent, based on the following. (1) The notion of infanatile sexuality as polymorphously perverse, and whose components constitute the 'core of our unconscious' and the ultimate 'matter' of unconscious desire. (2) The perspective of narcissism, which establishes desire, supported by the theory that maternity implants an illusion of completeness which culture promotes, despite the fact that such an ideal conflicts with the laws governing the foundation of culture. (3) The resulting forms of the oedipal complex and the castration complex, whether man or woman, imply a visible or unapparent violence with regard to the original 'call' of desire. (4) The traditional structures refer to the possibility of the mother imagining herself as completed by the child: the blocking of that illusion is associated with psychosis; the weakness of the desire, once established, demands, in the context of perversion, the presence of the figure of plenitude. Neurosis prefers its absence on the level of the apparent while insuring its permanence on the unconscious level.

Extra-Analytic Contacts: Fantasy and Reality

Ramon Ganzarain

Ramon Ganzarain

Ramon Ganzarain
Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute
1701 Uppergate Drive
Atlanta
Georgia 30322

Ramon Ganzarain  

SUMMARY

E-A.C. are avoided in our practice and literature. However, they often blur the distinction between transferential illusion and reality, more so in training analyses.

E-A.C. excite the patient's curiosity, exhibitionism and 'primal scene' fantasies with their concomitant anxieties. Such fears lead patients to phobically avoid analysing these encounters, whose effects analysts may also dread. However, calling attention to the avoidance may promote significant analytic developments.

Illusion on the psychoanalytic stage is a regressive force which brings out significant affects and internalized relationships. Affects focus the patient's attention. E-A.C. occur in a 'transitional space' where fantasy and reality become both confused and differentiated, allowing for playfulness, creativity and emotional growth.

The reality elements within psychoanalysis are reviewed: the patients' actual experience, the contract, the setting and the analyst's personality. The setting becomes the container of a mute symbiotic transference that may turn persecutory when the analytic routine is altered by an E-A.C. Hence, patients and analysts alike become anxious after an E-A.C.

E-A.C. reminds us that analysis—like life!—is a constant dynamic interplay between fantasy and reality.

The Wish for a Sex Change: A Challenge to Psychoanalysis?

Agnès Oppenheimer

Agnès Oppenheimer

Agnès Oppenheimer
134, rue de Grenelle
75007 Paris

Agnès Oppenheimer  

SUMMARY

This paper approaches different facets of male transsexualism from the point of view of different current psychoanalytic theories and the criticisms to which some of them give rise. On the basis of the author's observations, the determining psychological factor is stated to be the hatred of masculinity. The central thesis is that the clinical challenge made to psychoanalysis is an integral part of the pathology and that it highlights the working of this disorder, just as the 'reflection theories' of the manifest content of what the patients say represent in their objectifying reification a fundamental aspect of this

229

disturbance. Based on two clinical vignettes, an attempt at reconstruction is proposed, in which the narcissistic aspects of this pathology are emphasized. Current theory tends to stress the concepts of deficit and developmental arrest, which are generally inconsistent with those of conflict and defence, but they are here linked organically together.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A River can't be Green, can It? from the Analysis of Boris

Lore Schacht

Lore Schacht

Lore Schacht
7830 Emmendingen 13
Windenreute
Panoramastrasse 6/22
Germany

Lore Schacht  

INTRODUCTION

On a Saturday afternoon in late January 1987, the warm sunshine proclaiming that the worst of the winter was over, I held critical converse with myself as I wandered up and down the hills of the Kaiserstuhl, pondering over the sessions of the last three weeks of analysis with Boris. Had I timed my interpretations correctly? Had the formulations not been too direct? Had I prepared my interpretive work adequately? What, in his innermost being, could and would the boy make of what had been said? In the last session before this weekend, after the sequence of interpretations I was now reviewing so critically, he had hissed at me more or less as follows after a long, long silence: 'You cow!'

I had accepted this and answered with a question of my own: 'And what are you if I am a cow?'

Boris had replied without hesitation, as if it were the most natural thing in the world: 'A flower!'

To which I had responded: 'A flower with some prickles and thorns on its stalk!'

'Yes', had been his affirmative rejoinder.

I had then answered: 'And the cow must be careful not to tread on the flower, as after all the flower cannot run away.'

If I had had any idea that, already on the eve of this day of my doubts—that is, after the last session before the weekend—Boris had come to the end of an extremely alarming period of refusing food which had lasted for several weeks and for the first time shown a healthy appetite again, while appearing to his parents cheerful and as if released from a spell, I would certainly have wandered with a lighter heart through the almost genteel array of ochre-coloured vineyards and orchards.

BACKGROUND

When Boris's mother telephoned me asking for an urgent consultation for her son, she said, choosing her words precisely, that the onset of his spastic constipation for which she had been referred to me had coincided with an operation on his father's heart three years earlier, when Boris was a year old, and with an illness of her own when Boris was not quite 3. She had had a mastectomy for carcinoma of the breast. However, the chemotherapy prescribed after the operation had had to be interrupted because she had not tolerated it and had suffered from severe diarrhoea and vomiting.

Litza Guttieres-Green  

… If I were to pursue my mother when she went up to bed and she saw that I had stayed up to say goodnight to her in the corridor, I would not be allowed to stay at home any longer, I would be packed off to boarding school on the very next day, there was no doubt about it. Well, even if I had to throw myself from the window five minutes later, I would rather have it so. What I wanted now was Mother, was to say goodnight to her; I had gone too far along the path towards the fulfilment of that wish for there to be any turning back.

… I advanced noiselessly along the corridor, my heart pounding so intensely that I found it hard to move—but at least it was pounding not now with anxiety but with terror and joy … I darted forward … Then her face became clouded with anger, and she said not a word to me … Now she could hear my father coming up from the dressing room where he had gone to take off his clothes and, to forestall the wrath he would unleash upon me, she snarled: 'Be off with you, quickly, before your father sees you waiting there like a lunatic!' I merely repeated: 'Come and say goodnight to me', filled with terror as I saw the gleam of my father's candle already rising up the wall, while also taking advantage of his approach so as to blackmail Mother, hoping that she would not persist in her refusal but, to get rid of me before my father came, would say: 'Go back to your room, I will come'. It was too late: my father was standing before us. No one heard as I involuntarily murmured: 'It is all up with me!'

Marcel Proust, A la recherche du temps perdu ([translated from] Ed. Gallimard, 1954, Vol. I. pp.
33–36)

Negation of the difference between the sexes

My first memory of Pierre goes back more than eight years. I can see him now, sitting in front of me, leaning slightly forward, calm, cold and controlled. When I tritely asked him: 'What brings you to me?' he responded off the cuff, without looking at me: 'I am a homosexual. And I will tell you right away that I don't think that is normal! I have known it since I was 13, when a man seduced me. I have not had any other sexual experience, but only boys attract me and they are forbidden to me, I am not interested in adult men; women do not exist for me

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Basic Unity

Margaret Little

Margaret Little

Margaret Little
1 Mayfield, 72 London Road
Dunton Green, Sevenoaks, Kent TN13 2UG

Margaret Little  

Dear Mr Tuckett,

For my own self respect I must put on record while I'm still alive that I have at last seen a different side of the delusion of Basic Unity, when it is not discarded, but retained as absolute truth.

'The greatest evil in the world stems from the belief that you are right; that no matter what you do God is with you … there must always be room for reasonable doubt' (quotation from a novel The Taking of Agnes by Jennifer Potter, Cape, 1985). This belief falls short of one that we find in many psychotic patients, 'I am God'.

The holders of either are not amenable to analysis though they may seek it, sometimes from one analyst after another. Two sought it from me, each having an analyst in many cities.

I will add another quotation: In a letter to William Smith dated 9 January 1795, Edmund Burke wrote 'The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is that good men do nothing'.

From Procrustes, in the name of Moses, Mohammed, Christ, St Paul to Hitler, Marx, Lenin, Stalin etc. this has been seen in all sorts of aspects; at its worst bringing racism, religious wars, destruction, concentration camps, Apartheid, fundamentalism, and so forth.

This is of particular importance now in view of the present state of the world in general. The break up of the Communist Soviet bloc, the rise of Islam etc. It is very complex, as like everything else in human life, it is at least two sided; good often comes out of evil, and evil out of good.

I am no longer able myself to develop this, but I hope that I am not alone in this recognition and that others will develop it.

(This is a postscript to my paper 'On basic unity' published in 1959 in this journal.)

364

Robert Wallerstein  

Dear Sir:

In their letter, Sidney and Esther Fine refer to my citation of the research study they presented to the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society in 1988 in which they essayed to demonstrate a relatedness of theoretical perspective to technique, i.e. of theory-differences to discerned differences in clinical practice. Their results were largely positive, documenting that by and large adherents of four differing psychoanalytic theoretical perspectives could in most instances be identified through characteristic and expectably phrased interventions, all offered in response to the same clinical protocol of one patient's verbalizations during a single analytic hour.

May 1990

Current and Historical Perspectives on the Borderline Patient

Edited by Reuben Fine. New York: Brunner/Mazel. 1989. Pp. 434.

Henri Rey

Henri Rey

Henri Rey
L'Eau Coulée, Route de la Filatte, 27880 Les Portes-en-Ré, France

Henri Rey  

This book is the first number of a series entitled 'Monographs of the Society for Psychoanalytic Training'. As its title indicates, the book deals with the historical background as well as with current issues on the borderline concept. The first part reviews the historical background of psychiatry, divided at the time into psychosis and neurosis. It was mostly Kraeplinian psychiatry and the lot of schizophrenics was a fatalistic one. However, Harry Stack Sullivan (1931) came on the scene, using a form of psychoanalytical psychotherapy, who claimed remarkable results, very different from the Kraeplinian doom. Some praised him, others doubted the results. But nevertheless, things began to be different.

Manfred Bleuler, the son of Eugen, was much more optimistic about the treatment of schizophrenics after a lifetime spent with schizophrenics (1979). So, nevertheless, if not for the average psychiatrist who remained Kraeplinian, hope was beginning to dawn in some ways.

Part II, 'Theoretical perspectives', begins by dealing with the importance of something existing between schizophrenia on the one hand and neurosis or normality on the other.

Before dealing with the emergence of the borderline concept, a word must be said about Ernest Jones, the only non-American included in the book. In his address to the Institute of Psychiatry at Columbia University (1929), Ernest Jones praised American Psychiatry as being the only psychiatry worthwhile in the world as comparatively little existed in Europe. This advance was due to the part played by psychoanalysis, and in that address, Jones made the statement: 'All mental morbidity … is a state of schizophrenia'. This statement demands enlargement, of course, but seems to join a similar thought of Freud.

This is a collection of 16 papers by 14 authors, including Thomas Ogden, John Steiner, Frances Tustin, James Grotstein, David Rosenfeld, Joyce McDougall, Harold Searles, Renata Gaddini, Vamik Volkan, Peter Goldberg, Heitor DePaola and Christopher Bollas. The editors each contribute two articles. More than half the contributors are consultants to Boyer's treatment centre, and the bibliographies at the conclusion of each of the chapters contain an abundance of mutual citations, as well as frequent references to others such as Bick, Meltzer, Winnicott, Anzieu and Bion. The papers are thematically heterogeneous, and I suspect that the rationale for the volume is the shared affinity of the authors for each other's work and for the work of Melanie Klein and others of the British object-relations group, including Fairbairn, who is rarely mentioned, with his emphasis on the self, as well as a common interest in the treatment of more disturbed patients, rather than a wish to explore particular clinical or theoretical issues.

The editors, Boyer & Giovacchini, both believe that, in treating these patients who are fixated at what Boyer calls a prementational level of development, it is the analyst's ability to master his regressive countertransference response to the patient's projections which enables him to create a holding environment in which more mature structures may develop. Boyer presents two interesting case illustrations. Giovacchini describes a process of fusion epigenetically antecedent to achievement of the capacity to use projective-introjective mechanisms which characterizes both pathological and growth-promoting interchanges between patient and analyst.

Ogden utilizes Klein's developmental framework to construct three positions, the paranoid-schizoid, the depressive, and a primal position of his own invention, called autistic-contiguous, based on presymbolic sensory experience, which are presumed to exist in simultaneous dialectic relationship with one another. He is not so much concerned with the Kleinian defensive and developmental significance of these positions, as with ways in which they may combine to determine the cognitive, affective, and interpersonal flavour of the analytic dialogue. Ogden's contribution is followed by that of Steiner, who also focuses on Klein's 'positions', but in a much more traditional manner. Steiner, too, has added a third position; however his is intermediate, or 'borderline'. His clinical vignettes illustrate how a traditional Kleinian analyst conducts initial diagnostic interviews.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Equivalence Principle

Kenneth Epstein

Kenneth Epstein

Kenneth J. Epstein, Physicist
5252 Broadway 308
Chicago
Illinois 60640

Kenneth Epstein  

Dear Sir:

There is reason to hypothesize that the timeless symmetry of the unconscious may, in a very basic sense, be entirely equivalent to the timeoriented asymmetry of the conscious. The article 'From symmetry to asymmetry' by Klaus Fink (IJPA, 70:481–489), and the discussion of asymmetry through symmetry by Ignacio Matte-Blanco (IJPA, 70: 491–498), emphasize the fundamental nature and importance of these concepts. Their equivalence is suggested by considering the evolution of the universe as a paradigm for the evolution of the psyche—by considering the psyche as a microcosm of the cosmos.

According to the widely accepted cosmological model known as 'the big bang' (Parker, 1986, p. 157), the nascent universe went through a highly symmetric phase (the Planck era, named in honour of the physicist Max Planck who introduced the quantum) (Parker, 1987, p. 234). This brief phase of simple symmetry—exceedingly simple compared to the present state of the universe (Parker, 1986, p. 264) —was followed by a series of symmetry breakings, each of which introduced asymmetry and complexity to the physics of the cosmos.

When cosmos is replaced by psyche, the desired paradigm results. The nascent psyche has the simple symmetry of the unconscious. Through a series of symmetry breakings, the complex asymmetry of the conscious gradually evolves to the adult level.

In the cosmological model, both the symmetry and the symmetry breaking are quantum phenomena, from which it follows that the present asymmetric state of the universe is entirely equivalent to the early symmetric state. Nothing was lost or gained through broken symmetry.

Hence, in quantum psychodynamics (IRPA, 14: 125–126), nothing is lost or gained in transitions between conscious and unconscious thought. Regression to the unconscious level is reversible. Full recovery is possible. Regression in the service of the ego (often emphasized in psychology) is possible and constructive.

December 1989

Melanie Klein Today: Developments in Theory and Practice. Volume 2: Mainly Practice

Edited by Elizabeth Bott Spillius. London and New York: Routledge. 1988. Pp. 317.

Daniel Traub-Werner

Daniel Traub-Werner

Daniel Traub-Werner
602-586 Eglinton Avenue East, Toronto, Ont. Canada M4P 1P2

Daniel Traub-Werner  

This two-volume work, of what can be considered modern seminal papers in psychoanalytic thinking from well-known Kleinian authors, is divided into Volume 1: Mainly Theory and Volume 2: Mainly Practice. It is the latter which will be reviewed here.

In the general introduction Elizabeth Bott Spillius sets forth her intent as editor: to discuss papers which illustrate the development of Melanie Klein's concepts and formulations, as put forward by her followers in the past thirty years. The papers reveal a renewed interest among Kleinian scholars in issues of technique with transference, countertransference and projective identification as their main focus.

The volume is divided into four distinct sections: 'Developments in Technique', 'Clinical Papers on Adult Psychoanalysis', 'The Analysis of Children', and 'The Application of Kleinian Ideas in Other Fields of Work'. Each section is introduced by the editor and the papers are reviewed and placed into a general framework within an appropriate clinical, theoretical and historical context.

'Part One: Developments in Technique' consists of six classical papers, the first of which is Bion's 'Notes on memory and desire'. The paper outlines the Kleinian view that psychoanalytic observation should concern itself neither with the past nor with the future; rather, its focus ought to be on the interaction in the here and now.

[[RunningHead]]

Self-Psychology: Comparisons and Contrasts

By Douglas W. Detrick and Susan P. Detrick. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1989. Pp. 485.

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven T. Levy
1711 Uppergate Drive, Atlanta, GA 30322

Steven Levy  

It has been a common criticism of the work of Heinz Kohut and subsequent self-psychologists that they have, in their writings, failed to acknowledge, in any systematic manner, the contributions of other theorists and clinicians who have addressed similar issues in related ways. Kohut, in particular, has been faulted for what may appear as a lack of scholarship in relating his ideas to the work of his predecessors and colleagues. The current volume brings together a group of authors knowledgeable about both self-psychology and other related clinical and philosophical areas in order to place self-psychology within contemporary as well as historical psychoanalytic perspective.

The work is divided into five sections. The first addresses the relationship of self-psychology to the work of Freud and historical dissidents including Jung, Adler, Rank, Ferenczi and Horney. The general themes of these chapters are presented first by Michael Franz Basch in his contribution comparing Freud and Kohut. Freud is taken to task for his reliance on nineteenth-century scientific ideas, leading to a mechanistic theoretical bias, for his emphasis on biologically based instincts as the only motivating force in mental functioning, and for insisting on the centrality of conflict during the oedipal period of development in the generation of mental disturbances. Each of the dissidents is discussed in relation to Kohut in terms of these themes, as well as in relation to their taking up the issue of the self as a central mental structure. Each chapter has its own particular emphasis. Basch presents Kohut as a worker within the psychoanalytic mainstream whose contributions extend Freud's clinical discoveries, correct his outmoded metapsychological theorizing, and broaden his perspective on the development of psychopathology. The tone is one of integration rather than dissidence.

Corbett's chapter on Kohut and Jung emphasizes mainly Jung's focus on issues of the self. Stepansky's chapter on Adler and Kohut focuses on psychoanalysis as a research methodology. He particularly addresses whether an introspective-empathic clinical method falls within the psychoanalytic research tradition and whether it places psychoanalysis legitimately within the purview of science. Menaker emphasizes Rank's interest in the self, expressed in terms of his notion of the function of the will. The importance of self-development as the driving force motivating human action and creative expressiveness in Rank's work is the central linking element between his work and the self-psychological position. Rachman explores the contributions of Ferenczi, emphasizing his clinical experimentalism and its relationship to the role of empathy in clinical self-psychological work. Likewise, Ferenczi's interest in traumatic experiences during childhood and Kohut's view of the traumatic effects of non-empathic parenting are compared and contrasted. Rachman takes up the issue of resistance in analysis in terms of Ferenczi's novel and experimental clinical contributions and Kohut's emphasis on the curative aspects of the empathic psychoanalytic relationship. The author relates this issue to the humanistic tradition that Ferenczi pioneered in relation to psychoanalytic technique. Paul's contribution on Karen Horney summarizes her theoretical and technical positions and portrays them as essentially an independent self-psychology.

[[RunningHead]]

Analytical Dialogue and Psychic Change a Clinical Perspective

Jaime Lutenberg

Jaime Lutenberg

Jaime Marcos Lutenberg
Nuñez 3358
1429 Buenos Aires

Jaime Lutenberg  

INTRODUCTION

In this paper I endeavour to illustrate the clinical vicissitudes of the analytical relationship with a patient who at the time of the first consultation presented many problems of therapeutic approach, which were tackled in different ways. Practically all the changes which occurred in his internal and external world were the result of a fruitful psychoanalytic interchange that was made possible by analysis of the transference and countertransference.

I shall try to convey the dynamics of the relationship solely through the channels of the analytical dialogue itself. I shall not be advancing any theoretical hypothesis despite the many implications of the clinical material.

The paper is constructed as follows. I begin with a summary of the development of the therapeutic relationship and of the patient's history. I then reproduce two complete sessions separated by three months. For comparison, these two sessions are preceded by fragments of a session dating from three years earlier. The paper ends with a separate section containing some notes on the material presented.

HISTORY

I shall call the patient who is the subject of this paper José. He came for consultation on the recommendation of his family therapist. He did not seem to have any idea of the reasons for the recommendation. He lived with his wife and only son, aged 14. His wife had for some years been suggesting that they should separate because it had become impossible to live with him; José could not agree because, he said, he had nowhere else to live.

At the time of the consultation, the patient was 42 years old. When I showed him into the consulting room, I was faced with a tall, slim person, conspicuous by his dress, his clothes old and shabby, their colours dull and clashing. From what he said and from his general attitude, I realized that he was aware of the negative aesthetic impression he made on people.

He claimed that there was no cure for his trouble because it was due more to the shocking aesthetic appearance of his face than to the way he related to people, as I could no doubt see. He said that no change was possible in him, either physically or mentally. I could see from the outset that the patient was combining aesthetic, physical and mental problems, all of which were fused together by the apparent unmodifiability of his personal appearance. José thus confronted me with the problem of 'psychic change possible or impossible'. He felt himself to be incurable, but the analytical dialogue transformed this internal relational dilemma into a transference problem.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis: A Theory in Crisis

By Marshall Edelson. Chicago and London: University of Chicago Press. 1988. Pp. 376.

Adrienne Applegarth

Adrienne Applegarth

Adrienne Applegarth
1610 Scott Street, San Francisco, CA 94115

Adrienne Applegarth  

Among the controversies which have surrounded psychoanalysis since its beginnings, the dispute over whether this body of data and theory is to be classified as science has occupied a continuing and important place. Obviously, it has proved impossible to settle the issue to the satisfaction of everyone, which raises the question of why this is so. Perusal of the writings both of philosophers of science and psychoanalysts begins to make clear some of the reasons.

To begin with, there are marked differences of opinion about what is required to designate a field of inquiry as science. Concerning psychoanalysis, the difficulties centre around the nature of the data as obtained in the course of doing clinical psychoanalysis. This field of observation has made it difficult or impossible to provide data with the kinds of safeguards that most scientists are used to, e.g. consensual validation, replication, suitable controls, sufficient numbers to allow statistical analysis, simple predictions and opportunities to falsify propositions, etc.

The second major difficulty lies in the marked discrepancies in the accounts of the numerous participants in these controversies concerning what the fundamentals of psychoanalytic theory are which are to be evaluated. Each author seems to pick out and develop his own picture of what the essentials of the theory are, using as a basis very different degrees of breadth of theory. Thus, some restrict their account to early Freud, others to selected aspects of Freud's writings from all his phases of theory development, while still others utilize a broader view encompassing the work of many writers since Freud.

Recently, there seems to have been a surge of interest and work around these problems. Within psychoanalysis itself, a number of authors have taken the position on various grounds that analysis should be considered a hermeneutic discipline rather than one fitting into natural science. On the other hand, from the side of philosophers of science have come a number of challenges to psychoanalytic claims to being considered science. Most recently, Adolf Grünbaum, in his work The Foundations of Psychoanalysis: A Philosophical Critique1 has defended psychoanalysis both against its critics from the hermeneutic camp and those who criticize it on more traditional grounds as non-science. But, at the same time, he has mounted some challenges of his own which have received a great deal of recent attention. It is into this arena that Edelson's book has entered in very impressive style.

[[RunningHead]]

A Skin for Thought: Interviews with Gilbert Tarrab on Psychology and Psychoanalysis

By Didier Anzieu. London: Karnac Books. 1990. Pp. 167.

Roger Kennedy

Roger Kennedy

Roger Kennedy
The Cassel Hospital
1 Ham Common, Richmond, Surrey RW10 7JF

Roger Kennedy  

This is an interesting book made up of interviews with the French psychoanalyst Didier Anzieu conducted by Gilbert Tarrab, Professor in the Department of Administrative Sciences at Quebec University. The advantage of this format is that a wide range of topics are covered by Anzieu, including his own personal history, what led him into psychoanalysis, his views on analytic theory including the role of the body and the functioning of groups, political movements such as the events of May 1968 in which he was initially a participant, social and ecological questions and the nature of the creative process.

The disadvantage is that topics are rarely covered in much depth, you get a taste for a subject but you are left somewhat frustrated. In addition, in this case the interviewer tends to look for the more sensational aspects of Anzieu's involvements, sometimes to the detriment of rigorous arguments. However, this is probably necessary in order to liven up the conversations, particularly when the subject of them is not prone to exaggeration or to dramatic gesture. I certainly ended up being interested in Anzieu's ideas, and wanted to follow them up in more detail.

Anzieu describes, with honesty, how he probably became an analyst in order to take care of his emotionally fragile mother, who was prone to depressive breakdowns necessitating hospitalizations. He felt that he 'got away with' an ordinary neurosis because of his solid and warm relationship with his father. However, his mother's madness became a domestic reality. Furthermore, he had to replace a dead sister, and was smothered with his parent's anxiety.

I was not allowed to risk myself in the outside air without being smothered under several layers of clothing: sweater, overcoat, beret and scarf. The envelopes of care, concern, and warmth with which my parents surrounded me, one upon another, did not part from me even when I left home. I carried their load on my back. My vitality was hidden at the heart of an onion, under several outer coverings (p. 3).

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Migration and Exile

By León and Rebeca Grinberg. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1989. Pp. 230.

Judith Jackson

Judith Jackson

Judith Jackson
35 Crooked Usage, London N3 3EU

Judith Jackson  

It is surprising that it has taken so long for the first psychoanalytic study of the vicissitudes of the migratory process to be undertaken. León and Rebeca Grinberg, two distinguished Argentinian psychoanalysts, who themselves have lived and worked in three continents, more than fill this gap. They create a moving and illuminating account which links the internal migratory experience of psychic development and change with the whole gamut of experience, both normal and pathological, of making moves.

Throughout the book the reader is encouraged to investigate the upheavals that affect the individual's sense of identity. One is stimulated to consider the way each successive move, whether environmental or developmental, depends on an interplay between internal and external factors, fantasy and reality, introjective and projective identifications. This kind of interplay is very much at work in the process of migrating to a new country. On the one hand there are all the fears and hopes, fantasies and conflicts of the immigrant; on the other, there is the question of how he is welcomed and given a helping hand by the receiving country when he arrives. Underlying all of this is the question of why somebody chooses to make a move: what fuels his desire to leave his country? The writers examine the various permutations and splitting mechanisms that ensue.

The Emergence of Oedipal Centrality Comments on Michael Feldman's Paper 'Common Ground: The Centrality of the Oedipus Complex'

Robert Tyson

Robert Tyson

Robert L. Tyson
3252 Holiday Court, Suite 102
La Jolla
CA 92037

Robert Tyson  

SUMMARY

The Oedipus complex is indeed a central organizing focus of human development. Given the opportunity to review psychoanalytic work illustrative of how this concept influences clinical theory and technique, patches of common ground and of significant differences are described. The patient discussed was in the termination phase of a very long analysis, resulting in a concentration and telescoping of the three weeks of reported material from six months

42

prior to termination. This facilitated the elucidation of similarities and differences in viewing and handling manifestations of resistance, transference and countertransference. In particular, differences in conceptualization of the Oedipus complex were addressed, especially the constraints imposed on the analyst's thinking and technique by an obligatory perception that all material represented oedipal themes. In an alternative view, a pre-oedipal, dyadic developmental foundation provides a framework for the elaboration and evolution of oedipal, triadic complexities as a developmental achievement. This enables a reconsideration of transference manifestations of conflictual object relations in terms of distinctions between dyadic, pre-oedipal and triadic, oedipal themes, thus enhancing the analyst's interpretive relevance.

George Atwood  

Dear Mr Tuckett,

Early in his critical review of our (1987) book, Psychoanalytic Treatment: An Intersubjective Approach, Meyers (1990) declares, 'The authors are convinced of the superiority of the intersubjective viewpoint and have written a forthrightly polemical work in defence of it' (p. 178). We wish to make clear that our primary aim in writing the book was not to argue for the superiority of the intersubjective viewpoint; it was rather to call attention to the failure to investigate fully the impact of the observer on the observed in psychoanalysis, and to examine as well the implications of this neglect for our science. In so doing, we outlined an approach that, while retaining a fidelity to the psychoanalytic method of investigation, differs in essential ways from the more familiar ideas embraced by the dominant psychoanalytic schools with respect to motivation, psychopathology, psychic conflict and its relationship to developmental deficit, and, especially, the nature of the therapeutic process.

Meyers' review provides us with the opportunity to clarify certain aspects of our viewpoint that are frequently misunderstood. He feels, for example, that an 'unresolvable paradox' pervades our work because, on one hand, we claim that all psychoanalytic understanding develops from dialogue between the personal, subjective worlds of analyst and patient, while, on the other hand, we offer numerous clinical generalizations throughout the book. Meyers sees this as a paradox because he mistakenly equates our position with an injunction that the analyst must not have any theoretical ideas or beliefs of his own, a notion that would be entirely at variance with our view of the psychoanalytic situation as an intersubjective system to which the psychological organizations of both analyst and patient contribute. What we do assert, in a passage quoted by Meyers, is that the analyst's theoretical framework should not be elevated to the status of objective fact. Any analyst's theoretical preferences (including our own) are part of his personal subjectivity, rooted in his formative life experiences (Stolorow & Atwood, 1979). It is central to our viewpoint that the analyst's personal organizing principles can never be eliminated from the analytic system. However, it is essential that the analyst continually reflect on the operation of these principles, including those enshrined in his theories, so that their codetermining impact on the course of the analytic process—how their manifestations are, in turn, given exquisitely personal meanings by the patient—can be investigated and understood. There is no paradox here, only sustained empathic-introspective inquiry.

Thinking about Thinking: Some Clinical and Theoretical Considerations in the Treatment of a Borderline Patient

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy
Anna Freud Centre
21 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3

Peter Fonagy  

SUMMARY

This paper addresses a specific aspect of pathological mental functioning in so-called borderline patients. Analytic work with a borderline man is presented to show that an inhibition of, and defences against, the contemplation of one's own and others' mental states may be a hallmark of the resistance encountered in a number of

653

such patients. It is claimed that the analysis of transference and countertransference is a crucial therapeutic factor in tackling this source of resistance. In doing this, the paper draws upon a topical notion from philosophy of mind and recent ideas from child development studies which help to clarify psychoanalytic ideas concerning the nature of the pathology of internal object relations underlying feelings of emptiness and social alienation in borderline functioning.

SUMMARY

Pseudologia fantastica is discussed from the classical viewpoint as a defensive effort based primarily on denial and directed towards unacceptable wishes and impulses. This view is contrasted with British object relations theory which stresses schizoid and dissociative elements and the function of pseudologia fantastica in maintaining a viable self. While appearing at times to reflect creativity, pseudologia fantastica in fact constitutes a failure of potential space. The treatment of an adolescent boy is presented to illustrate the technical necessity of providing a holding and containing function which allows for the gradual emergence of potential space. As the patient's self develops, the treatment may eventually address internal conflicts typical of adolescence.

Encapsulation as a Defence Against the Fear of Annihilation

Earl Hopper

Earl Hopper

Earl Hopper
12 Heath Mansions
The Mount
London NW3 6SN

Earl Hopper  

SUMMARY

Encapsulation is defined as a defence against annihilation anxiety. A theoretical orientation concerning the origins of this kind of anxiety is outlined, and the causal efficacy of early traumata is emphasized. On this basis, encapsulation is conceptualized as an introjective process through which attempts are made to fuse and confuse parts of the self with parts of the lost and abandoning object. Clinical and case history data from several patients are presented. A number of topics for further research are suggested. The literature is reviewed, and the current use of 'encapsulation' is linked to earlier work on disavowal, foreclosure and resourceless dependence.

WHITHER THE PSYCHOANALYTIC METHOD?

Ms W, 29 years old and single, entered psychoanalysis for help with a problem of compulsive promiscuity. With the exception of this serious symptom, her life was successful. She was doing well in her chosen occupation, had many good friends, and led, in other respects, a fulfilling life. Further evaluation led me to conclude that her symptoms were evidence of a psychoneurotic character disorder, hysterical type.

Ms W radiated charm. Though dressed with subdued elegance, she managed at the same time to project an image that was alluring and provocative. Several other of my women patients who encountered her going to and from her appointments with me were alternately angry with and envious of her, and the colleagues with whom I shared a waiting room uncharacteristically found themselves commenting to me on this patient. It was this sort of an influence that she exercised wherever she went. The targets of her attention were usually older, successful men, in leadership positions, and they readily succumbed to her. Predictably, she unleashed the full force of her seductive personality in the analysis. I, however, had no difficulty in maintaining a thoughtful neutrality and a non-intervening, freely-hovering attention that promoted the development of a classic oedipal transference. In due time, the interpretation of that transference and its resolution through genetic reconstruction freed Ms W from her neurosis. A result that attested once again to the fact that the psychoanalytic method, an approach summed up by the terms neutrality, abstinence, and empathy (Basch, 1983), is as valid today as it was when Freud first described it (Freud, 1912a), (1912b), (1913), (1914), (1915).

The question I now want to pose is the following: Why was I, at least initially, not flustered, preoccupied with fantasies, struggling to resist temptation, and so forth, when this undeniably charming, sophisticated, and attractive woman made me the object of her desire? How was I able to maintain analytic equanimity, and focus on deciphering the hidden messages behind her seductive behaviour that, outside the analytic consulting room, would certainly have had some effect on me personally?

[[RunningHead]]

From Raging Bull to Theseus; The Long Path of a Transformation

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro
Via Cardano, 77
27100 Pavia
Italy

Antonino Ferro  

Natalino—so named because, he told me, he was born on Christmas Day (Natale in Italian)—was 24 years old when his analysis began; he came from a family with a country background and was the youngest of seven siblings. We began the analysis, now in its ninth year, at four sessions a week, after three or four preliminary meetings.

It is difficult to convey a unified view of the patient because of the tangle of different clinical pictures he presented, as if his mind were 'in pieces' (in all senses of the term), each of which so to speak needed adjusting on its own account before reassembly with the others.

The 'story' was that of a child who had formed a symbiotic relationship with a seriously depressed mother; when his mother died during his teens, he at first exhibited obsessional symptoms of increasing severity, but this defence eventually failed, giving way to a delusional state between persecution and a desire for restoration of his rights.

The beginning of the analysis and the whole of the first two years were marked by terrible violence and anxiety; the patient could not bear either to lie or to sit on the couch. I stayed in my position, sitting in my armchair, in 'my usual analyst's place', while he sat in a small armchair in a corner of the room, more or less opposite my chair.1

It seemed like the positions taken up by boxers in a ring, and the brother, the violent and destructive part of himself, who answered to the nickname of 'Raging Bull' or 'Jake La Motta'—or rather, as I experienced him, 'Jack the Ripper'—was indeed a boxer.

The first long months were a time of terror, for him and certainly for me too, at Natalino's terrible violence and his suicidal designs—'but only after a massacre', so that he and the evil that had been done to him would be publicized in the papers, a massacre that would wipe out all of his family and, of course, me as well.

Fortunately, I, too, had a 'boxer brother', that is, a psychiatrist colleague who would immediately, subject to the patient's consent, give him drug treatment; he has always worked with me for as long as drugs have been necessary.
