The Body as a Stage for Criminal Acting out

Moisés Rabih

Moisés Rabih

Moisés Rabih
Jose E. Uriburu 1690-P. 19-Dto A (1114)
Capital Federal
Republic Argentina

Moisés Rabih  

INTRODUCTION

In this paper I will present case material relating to a period of my analysis of a severely disturbed patient in an attempt to demonstrate my understanding of the patient and also the techniques and operating styles that shaped my attempts to establish contact with him.

I will attempt to centre this paper on our analytical dialogue, with special emphasis on my countertransference since this proved an invaluable aid in tackling this extremely difficult patient. Our analysis sessions were a constant struggle: he came to me four times a week and caused me such continual worry that it was very difficult for me not to think about him all the time. He aroused a whole range of very different emotions in me, including affection, fear, hostility, etc. Sometimes I even experienced periods of intense paranoia when I feared the patient was at the point of attacking me and losing control. This was offset on other occasions by my fondness for him. I felt a desire to help him when I was able to contact his more needy, defenceless 'patient side'—but this unfortunately happened only rarely.

I propose to follow a few explanatory notes relating to my material with a description of a certain period of analysis. This covers the vicissitudes experienced by my patient and myself as a result of a severe episode of acting out, which was expressed bodily and displayed disconcerting, plainly psychotic features. This and other forms of behaviour expressed by the patient affected me so deeply that at some moments—fortunately short-lived—I was led to question my identity and ability as an analyst.

I would like to run quickly through some important moments of the first stage (one and a half years) of this difficult process before concentrating my clinical discussion on the severe episode of acting out mentioned above—I will refer to this as the 'surgical operation episode'. This episode of acting out was no more or less severe than others that occurred during our analysis. I chose this material because I thought it was interesting that the patient unconsciously chose his own body as the setting for a drama he could neither experience mentally nor externalize.

Judith Welles and Harriet Wrye

Harriet Wrye

Judith K. Welles
860 Via de la Paz
Pacific Palisades

Harriet Kimble Wrye
LOS ANGELES

Harriet Wrye  

SUMMARY

This paper reviews literature on countertransference erotism and perversion, and offers our conception of the maternal erotic countertransference. We stress that sado-masochistic issues are not only issues of aggression, but represent pre-oedipal erotism as well. The analyst's struggle with these issues may contribute to a perverse misalliance instead of a creative coupling. It is typical of the perverse misalliance that it contains a refusal to participate, with all the attendant disinterest and deadness and lack of creativity usually associated with that condition.

We have emphasized two keys to springing the analyst from the deadlocks of such paralysing countertransferences, thereby opening access to the creative use of both maternal erotic transference and countertransference. First, is the analyst's ability to view herself or himself as maternally erotic; and second, is the analyst's ability to experience, contain and make accessible to analysis the patient's potential erotism, including its perverse aspects.

Understanding Psychic Change in Child Psychoanalysis

Peter Fonagy and George Moran

George Moran

Peter Fonagy
George S. Moran
12, 14 & 21 Maresfield Gardens

George Moran  

Psychoanalysts are impressed by differences between patients in terms of the rate at which psychic change occurs. They are aware that an individual's capacity for change is difficult to predict on the basis of symptoms and history alone. In trying to understand why some patients change relatively quickly whilst others are refractory to treatment, numerous theoretical constructs related to the nature of pathology have been put forward. These include pathological internalized object relations, pathological unconscious fantasy, early trauma, adhesiveness of libidinal fixations, imbalances in defensive structure, ego restrictions, deficits in ego functioning and object relatedness and pathological psychic organizations. The multiplicity of available accounts to explain the variability of the rate of psychic change in itself suggests the need to reexamine the issue. In this paper we wish to complement our developmental psychoanalytic approach with a cognitive perspective in an attempt to understand the problem of the rate and stability of psychic change.

THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN DEVELOPMENTAL AND CONFLICTUAL PSYCHOPATHOLOGY

Analytic work with young children gives an opportunity to observe early mental functioning in the process of development and to witness how infantile elements of the child's mental life come to be transformed and integrated.

We wish to thank Professor Joseph Sandler, Mrs Hansi Kennedy and Mrs Audrey Gavshon for their helpful suggestions on earlier drafts of his paper. However, responsibility for the ideas put forward is assumed by the authors.

Revised Understandings of Psychogenic Autism

Frances Tustin

Frances Tustin

Frances Tustin
LONDON
17 Orchard Lane
Amersham
Bucks HP6 5AA

Frances Tustin  

SUMMARY

The author suggests that, in the light of observational findings from babies, it is no longer tenable to postulate a normal autistic phase in infancy, and to view pathological autism as a halt at, or a regression to, such a phase. This has been a commonly held psychoanalytic view concerning the aetiology of autism, and she has subscribed to it. In this paper, the author presents a revision of these views, in that she now reserves the term autism to refer solely to pathological conditions. Her present view is that autism is a system of protective, but alienating auto-sensual aberrations which have developed to deal with an infantile trauma of seeming to be wrenched away from a mothering person's body. She suggests that in an unduly fused nursing situation, mother and child had felt undifferentiated from each other. Thus, the infant had

589

taken it for granted that the mother's body was a permanent part of 'its' body. It was an unbearable shock when this was found to be not so. The author suggests that autistic reactions to this shock are the source of the autistic child's many deficits. Based on clinical material presented in her other books and papers, she has attempted to describe and conceptualize the protective auto-sensual reactive aberrations which develop to deal with this traumatic awareness of bodily separateness.

A. Limentani

38 Penshurst Gardens
Edgware

A. Limentani  

SUMMARY

Current researches in sexual deviations have rightly stressed deficiencies and faults in the mother/child relationship, fears of annihilation and an inability to achieve separation and independence. As a result of this the role of the father in the patient's family structure and in their psychotherapies and psychoanalyses has been somewhat neglected, also because the fathers are usually described as cold, distant, uninvolved, etc. The author believes that the obliteration of the father figure, whether accidental or deliberate, is an important part of the psychopathology. It follows that in the late stages of a therapy there should be a careful examination of the original establishment of the father as a second object, as its internalization is essential for a healthy sexual development. The obliteration of the father figure can cause serious problems in terms of the understanding of the transference and countertransference. The paper includes a clinical description of a case where such problems were much in evidence.

Clinical Case Description of a Segment of a Psychoanalytic Experience

Scott Carder

Scott Carder

Scott L. Carder
2400 Mission Street
San Marino
CA 91108

Scott Carder  

INTRODUCTION

This paper is a response to the invitation for clinical accounts by the editors of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis(Tuckett & Hayley, 1990). That invitation is part of an attempt to search for common ground among psychoanalysts. The report of this case and the author's understanding of it, is an effort to communicate at the level of clinical experience. The author demonstrates how he works psychoanalytically with a particular person. Hopefully, this case reflects his general psychoanalytic work and has some common aspects that will be familiar to other psychoanalysts in their work. This narrative reconstruction of a brief part of an analysis will attempt to show the 'art' of psychoanalysis which is intuitively to blend the analytic, insight-enhancing procedures, with all other non-analytic but therapeutically necessary activities (Greenson, 1967).

The discussion portion of the paper will summarize the psychoanalytic process, the transference and resistance aspects of this treatment. The author will also briefly discuss the theoretical views which are most helpful to him. Psychoanalysis as a reparenting process will be highlighted using material most closely related to the recent work of John Bowlby, as shown in his book, A Secure Base(1988).

CASE DESCRIPTION

A. The Analyst: He was a 47-year-old psychiatrist who graduated from his analytic institute eight years before this treatment began. About half of his clinical practice for the previous five years or more had been doing psychoanalysis on a four or five time weekly basis. At the time this case began, he had four cases in analysis, all at five sessions per week. This information is provided to help orient the reader to the analyst and his experience with psychoanalysis.

P. The Patient: Mrs C was a 40-year-old caucasian woman who, at the time of beginning analysis, had been married for 15 years to her husband, John, who was a significantly older man. The couple had elected not to have children. Mrs C was a housewife who had graduated from a fine arts college and now worked as an artist, while Mr C was a businessman who had been talking of retiring for the past few years.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis: The Common Ground

Sidney Fine M.D. and Esther Fine

Esther Fine

Sidney Fine, M.D.
Esther Fine, L.C.S.W.
1800 Fairburn Avenue
Los Angeles
CA 90025

Esther Fine  

Dear Sir:

As Dr Robert Wallerstein reported in his paper, 'Psychoanalysis: the common ground' (International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, Volume 71, Part 1, 1990), our study clearly indicated that there are significant differences at least among four major theoretical groups in psychoanalysis in the areas of the interpretation of transference, of defense and resistance, of aggression, of primitive impulses, in the sensitivity to the patient's vulnerability, and the use of characteristic language.

We concluded our paper with the question: 'What difference do these differences make?' We answered it by stating that this was a problem for future researchers to resolve, using systematic, scientific methodology. We do not believe that definitive conclusions can be drawn from anecdotal clinical material, authoritative opinions, or armchair theorizing.

In the last analysis, the effect of theoretical orientation on clinical practice and therapeutic outcome must be based on sound research findings, not on the powers of persuasive oratory.

May 1990

SUMMARY

Clinical material is presented to illustrate the difference between psychotic and non-psychotic organizations of the personality. It is suggested that the psychotic part of the personality strives

206

to possess and control its objects because it needs them to repair a damaged ego. The non-psychotic part on the other hand is able to face reality sufficiently to relinquish its objects and mourn them. The interaction between the two parts in the same patient is discussed.

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

Glen O. Gabbard
The Menninger Clinic
Box 829
Topeka
Kansas 66601

Glen Gabbard  

SUMMARY

Transference hate presents a major obstacle to effective analytic work with borderline patients. In a subgroup of these patients, the analyst is hated relentlessly in a manner that seems unresponsive to interpretation. The persistent projective identification of hated aspects of the patient's internal world may erode the analyst's ability to maintain his analytic posture and lead to various forms of countertransference acting out. A prolonged period of containment is required for both patient and analyst before they are likely to converge in an 'analytic space' where interpretation will be effective.

SUMMARY

The paper discusses the positive and the negative sides of the common ground perspectives as proposed at the Montreal and Rome Congresses. Freud is the origin of the guardian role of the IPA, but the time is over when one man or one small group could dictate what psychoanalysis is and should be. This task has become

61

an ongoing challenge for the total group. Psychoanalysis has to avoid becoming a fourre-tout as well as avoiding being ruled by any sort of dictatorial credo. One ideal way to deal with the unavoidable basic divergences is what Roy Schafer has called comparative psychoanalysis, where each new approach is evaluated in respect to the existing fundamental principles; in the long run, the irrelevant views would fade away. In the second part, the author gives his own idea about the irreducible principles that should define essential psychoanalysis.

SUMMARY

This paper describes a particular form of 'silent' regression during the psychoanalytic treatment of infantile personalities (a type of character pathology related to the hysterical personality). This regression is characterized by the development of rapid interchange in the roles enacted in the transference and projected on to the analyst, an apparent 'disconnexion' of the transference material from that dominant when the patient is in non-regressed states, and the intensification of the analyst's countertransference reactions. The diagnosis and management of these regressive transferences requires a particular interpretive style and strategy outlined in this paper and illustrated with two case vignettes.

Adolescence and the Repetition Compulsion

François Ladame

François Ladame

François Ladame
16-18 Bd. St George
1211 Genève 8
Switzerland

François Ladame  

SUMMARY

I describe the connexion between the failure of the procedures of adolescence and the establishment of a psychical economy dominated by the repetition compulsion.

The concept of the repetition compulsion appears as early as in 1914, in 'Remembering, repeating and working-through', but is not clearly specified as a characteristic of instinctual functioning until 1919 and 1920, in 'The "Uncanny"' and 'Beyond the pleasure principle'.

In these two works, Freud presents what amounts to a model of the disorganization of the mental apparatus observed in 'breaks' in the adolescent process when the capacity to bind instinctual excitation in the primary process is swamped. Decompensation is precipitated by the change in the reality principle (de facto infantile impotence becoming adult potency).

The repetition compulsion, in the strict sense of the term, is a matter of 'crude', unbound instincts. Moreover, the idea emerges from Freud's two texts that the instinctual demand manifested through the repetition compulsion is the persistence of the incestuous and parricidal wish. This is borne out by my analytical experience, as the two treatments I adduce to illustrate the theory show.

SUMMARY

A case is described in which a patient's dream of red shoes stimulated associations to the Hans Christian Andersen fairy tale of The Red Shoes, and the movie of the same name. Analysis of the dream provided valuable insight into the structure and evolution of her neurotic conflicts. At the same time, the symbolic significance of The Red Shoes to this patient helped to clarify one of the meanings of the fairy tale. Marilia Aisenstein and Alain Gibeault

Alain Gibeault

Marilia Aisenstein
72 rue d'Assas
75006 Paris
Alain Gibeault
17 rue Albert Bayet
75013 Paris

Alain Gibeault  

SUMMARY

The authors present a metapsychological conception of hypochondria. Following Freud, they contrast the complaints of the hypochondriac with the belle indifférence of the hysteric, and they then enquire into the heuristic value of hypochondria as an actual neurosis; this leads them to a consideration of psychosomatic illness and the importance of the object cathexis in hypochondriacal anxiety. In the development of Freud's theory of the drives, the explanatory concept of the damming up of ego libido proves insufficient and has to be coupled with the notion of primary erotogenic masochism: from this point of view, hypochondria can be seen as a form of binding which thus distinguishes it from other somatic outcomes. On the basis of three case histories, the authors endeavour to show hypochondriacal anxiety not only as a pathological process but also as a type of necessary minimum cathexis of the body; this leads to the idea of hypochondria as psychical work.

responseSet.title: The Subject of Mind
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Subject of Mind

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell
1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley
CA 94708

Marcia Cavell  

SUMMARY

Though certain assumptions about the nature of meaning that I call subjectivist go contrary to the general direction of psychoanalytic thought, they are intrinsic to Freud's view of the unconscious as a special mental system with characteristics unique to it. A corollary to this view of meaning in Freud's case is that instinctual processes are a rudimentary form of the mental, providing a genetic base from which 'secondary' mental states derive. I argue that the mental is irreducible to instinct or anything else, and that even in its most rudimentary form, mentality presumes an interaction between creature and the external world, also creature and other creatures, that has an essentially linguistic nature. I claim that psychoanalysis would lose nothing that is crucial to it were it to view subjectivity as something that can only emerge in time; on the contrary it would clarify its own most valuable insights.

Freud's Irma Dream: A Psychoanalytic Interpretation

Barbara Mautner

Barbara Mautner

Barbara Mautner
The Apthorp
2211 Broadway – Suite 4L
New York
NY 10024

Barbara Mautner  

SUMMARY

This paper presents the hypothesis that Freud may have been haunted by the repressed memory of an incident of erotic aggression enacted by himself against his sister Anna when he was 5 years old and she 3 years old. It is suggested that this experience and its psychic ramifications make up the latent content of the Irma dream.

The Irma dream is viewed here as the first dream of Freud's self-analysis. As in the analysis of the first reported dream in an actual treatment situation, meaning emerges progressively over time. The interpretation proposed in this paper is based on data emerging from 1895 until 1924. Principal

285

sources are 'The interpretation of dreams', which is seen as an associative monologue, the letters to Wilhelm Fliess and Freud's early theoretical works.

SUMMARY

This paper presents a summary and an overview of the 36th IPA Congress, 'Common ground in psychoanalysis: clinical aims and processes'. The theme emerged from Dr Robert Wallerstein's 1987 Montreal Congress Plenary Address, 'One psychoanalysis or many'. The paper focuses on the three Rome Plenary presentations and their discussions, Wallerstein's presidential address, and the final panel review. A paper presented at the meeting by Charles Hanly, 'The concept of truth in psychoanalysis', which outlines theories of truth: correspondence versus coherence, provides the conceptual tools for considering the different points of view. The author shares Hanly's support for a pragmatically

54

qualified commitment to a correspondence theory of truth.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Penis Envy: Libidinal Metaphor and Experiential Metonym

Sallye Wilkinson

Sallye Wilkinson

Sallye M. Wilkinson
The Menninger Foundation
Topeka
Kansas

Sallye Wilkinson  

SUMMARY

The subjective narrative employed by the little girl to account for her femininity draws on her primary sense of femaleness, as well as on any secondary elaborations. The secondary nature of femininity is highlighted by the Freudian view wherein femininity is thought to be achieved through changes in libidinal aims and objects. The primary nature of femininity is highlighted by current views elaborating on ego development.

An examination of the subjective role of the phallus in a number of clinical reports reveals that envy of the penis may represent the intersection of several associative chains. For example, the girl's castration reaction could result from a condensation of the libidinal theme of not having a penis as Freud argued. However, Freud's reliance on the phallus as a metaphoric representation of instinctual vicissitudes is not an end point. In addition to Freud's elaborations of the phallic theme, the desire to possess the penis may represent the girl's associations to contiguous events. That is, through the process of displacement, the phallus also functions as a metonymic symbol. To fully appreciate penis envy, both the metaphoric and metonymic meanings assigned to femininity must be analysed.

Shame in Psychoanalysis: The Function of Unconscious Fantasies

Ana-Maria Rizzuto

Ana-Maria Rizzuto

Ana-Maria Rizzuto
75 Gardner Road
Brookline
MA 02146

Ana-Maria Rizzuto  

SUMMARY

The conceptualization of shame as an emergent affect in the clinical situation gains clarity by linking it to unconscious fantasies. The author suggests that shame appears when an individual fails in his efforts to obtain from another person, in reality or in imagined scenes, an expected similar affect or a complementary message, or both. A self-evaluation emerges of being undeserving of the desired response. In this respect shame is related to the narcissistic component of any experience or fantasy, be it pre-oedipal, oedipal, or post-oedipal. In cases where there is a pathological predisposition to shame, the predisposition is linked to unconscious fantasies portraying the individual as the desiring and frustrated subject of an unresponded to affective message. After its formation the unconscious fantasy itself, and all its unrepressed derivatives, become a source of shame. A clinical example illustrates these points.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Relationship Between Extratransference Interpretations and Transference Interpretations: A Clinical Study

Ludwig Haesler

Ludwig Haesler

Ludwig Haesler
6238 Hofheim (Taunas)
Sulzbacher Weg 5

Ludwig Haesler  

Since Strachey's fundamental paper of 1934 with its central postulate that only transference interpretations have 'mutative' effects, the question of the position and significance of transference interpretation in the analytical process has dominated the debate about the theory and technique of psychoanalysis. On the other hand, conspicuously little attention has been paid to the question of the position and significance of non-transference interpretations or extratransference interpretations, i.e. following Gill's (1982) classification, the interpretation of current situations outside the relationship with the analyst, as well as genetic interpretation. The more importance was attributed to interpretation of the transference and to the here and now, the greater was the inclination to regard other interpretations as performing solely a complementary and supporting function in the sense of promoting and preparing the 'actual' transference interpretation, and some even adopted positions whereby interpretations not directly related to the transference and to the here and now were regarded as superfluous, ineffective or even detrimental because they could not but distract from the 'essence' of the analytical process, namely, analysis of the transference and of resistance. However, such extreme 'transference-only' positions have remained controversial to this day.

It is certainly accepted that every analyst in his daily practice gives not only interpretations directed towards the transference and the here and now but also interpretations which concern situations and relationships outside the analysis in the present and in the past, and that this need not necessarily be immediately regarded as a displacement or flight from the transference. Strachey himself, despite the significance he attributed to transference interpretation, regarded such interpretations as 'none the less essential' (Strachey, 1934, p. 290). Yet, apart from the comprehensive contribution of Blum (1981) and a report of a panel discussion on the subject (Halpert, 1984), we still lack a sufficiently systematic theoretical foundation for non-transference interpretation (see also Blum, 1981).

Dear Sir,

Despite the strenuous efforts that both Merton Gill and I characteristically make towards the utmost of clarity in the presentations of our views and despite the mutual goodwill and serious intention that we bring to understanding the nature of our agreements and our disagreements, it seems that we each feel that the other seriously misunderstands and misrepresents his viewpoint. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Internalization of the Absent Father

Harwant Gill

Harwant Gill

Harwant S. Gill
Tavistock Clinic
120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA

Harwant Gill  

SUMMARY

The analysis of a woman, born of an unmarried mother, is presented. Her mental representation of the fantasized father was derived partly from her own wishes and fears, partly from what she was told by her mother, and partly from what she discovered through her own observations and enquiries. The revival in the transference of the split-off parts of her self and of the related bipolarity in her representation of the absent father, the search for their origin in her childhood, the discussion of their operation in her present and past relationships, and the working through of her resistances to change, enabled her to integrate the split-off parts of her self, and to maintain stable relationships.

The child's need to internalize a father,

251

whether or not the latter exists in reality, raises the question whether human offspring have an innate propensity to relate to a father, or a father introject, even when the latter is derived from fantasy alone.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein  

Dear Sir:

I agree very much with Merton Gill that our exchanges of letters have clarified the very substantial areas where we do agree and also the remaining and substantial areas where we continue to disagree. I think that I can respond to his reply to my response to his first letter quite briefly.

To respond sequentially: We are in full accord about the centrality of the interpersonal aspects of the psychoanalytic situation, and I did state in my response to Gill's first letter that the developing redefinition of the transference in this two-body or interactional directive is something that Gill has played a major role in expounding, and has done so very persuasively.

We are equally in accord that I do not now, nor have I ever, felt that Gill has either ever accepted or returned to Alexander's conception of the 'corrective emotional experience' which both Gill and I have seen as an attempted manipulation of the transference relationship which has no place in psychoanalysis proper, whatever place some may find for it in some psychotherapies. I feel that I have been very clear about this in my written comments both about Gill's views as of his 1954 paper and in his most recent papers. I feel, rather, that Gill confused this point at first, thinking that I thought his recent papers represented 'a return to Alexander' on this point; that misconception however has now been clarified.

Where Gill and I still do disagree are on two main issues. First, I do feel that he has returned to Alexander in a different regard, in what I consider a blurring of the relationship between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy in his most recent writings (and in contrast to his 1954 paper where he tried so hard to counteract Alexander's efforts to amalgamate psychoanalytic psychotherapy to psychoanalysis by clarifying and sharpening the conceptual boundaries between them). The nub of our difference is Gill's current belief that any therapy that tries to analyze the transference as completely and thoroughly as possible is psychoanalysis, no matter what the conditions or dimensions of the treatment (what he calls 'extrinsic criteria').

[[RunningHead]]

The Matrix of the Mind. Object Relations in the Psychoanalytic Dialogue

By Thomas H. Ogden. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1986. Pp. 270.

Joel Rosen

Joel Rosen

Joel E. Rosen
Austen Riggs Center
Stockbridge, Mass. 01262

Joel Rosen  

This is one of several books in the last several years that attempts to bring the insights of the British School of object relations into the contemporary American psychoanalytic scene. Volumes which have recently enlivened this debate include Mitchell & Greenberg's Object Relations in Psychoanalytic Theory(1983), as well as Judith Hughes' Reshaping the Psychoanalytic Domain(1989). What is distinct about Ogden's enterprise is his explicit aim to open our minds to what is valuable while pointing out potential blind spots. Towards this end, he includes rich vignettes which demonstrate the clinical relevance of the Britons' insights.

Ogden divides the book into three main sections. In the first chapter he defines his argument, while in the next three chapters he enters into a dialogue with Melanie Klein and tries to extricate and critique her unique clinical contributions. In the last third of the book he plays with Winnicott's contributions and demonstrates both the originality and utility of Winnicott's thought.

Eric Trist

Eric Trist
Professor Emeritus of Organizational Behavior and Social Ecology
130 NW 28th Street, Gainesville, Florida 32607

Eric Trist  

Whether or not they always agreed with him, all the many people who have worked with Tom Main during his time with the British Army in World War II, at the Cassel Hospital, which he was elected to direct immediately afterwards and where he stayed for 30 years, or in the Institute for Psycho-Sexual Medicine of which he was then made Life President, have been profoundly affected by his example. His tolerant but forceful charisma, the intellectual cogency and practical effectiveness of his ideas and what I can only call his therapeutic voice, left their mark on the half-dozen outstanding psychiatrists who led the innovations in psychiatry during World War II. His influence world-wide has grown with the years. His career models what one would expect of an analytical social psychiatrist. A brilliant presenter, he did not espouse being a writer. One or two of his papers have become famous, but the kernel of his broad message and its deep coherence over a wide array of activities will become extensively known only now after the publication of this, his one book. (A less modest title might have been Thinking Out and Opening up a Comprehensive Psychoanalytic Social Psychiatry.)

Developmental Breakdown and Psychoanalytic Treatment in Adolescence: Clinical Studies

By M. Laufer and M. E. Laufer. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1989. Pp. 217.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert M. Galatzer-Levy
180 North Michigan Avenue, Chicago, IL 60601

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

Psychoanalytic explorations of adolescence are as old as the field itself. From Freud's first investigations of the psychology of hysteria, through the development of ego psychology and the related concept of identity, to the emergence of separation-individuation theory and self psychology, psychoanalysts have learned much from their adolescent patients. Psychoanalytic theories about adolescence have ranged from Freud's formulation that intensified libidinal forces result in a resurgence of oedipal conflicts, to concepts of the reshaping of the ego, superego and identity, to theories centring on the adolescent self either in terms of continuing separation from parents or transformations of the nature of selfobjects.

Developmental Breakdown and Psychoanalytic Treatment in Adolescence continues the process in which adolescent patients enrich psychoanalytic understanding of human psychology and psychoanalytic formulations enhance our picture of this developmental era. The Laufers and their co-workers, in an important series of papers and books, have already contributed an exceptionally rich picture of adolescent development. Their work centres on the psychological meaning of the transformation of the adolescent body that has become physically capable of what could only be fantasized in childhood. The adolescent's central task, in the Laufers' view, is the psychological integration of the newly sexually and physically capable body. Turmoil of adolescence, which the Laufers, like most psychoanalysts but unlike many developmental psychologists, regard as normative, is an ordinary manifestation of this process. Since it is a new body that must be psychologically integrated, it is not surprising that action on or about this body is central to adolescent psychology. Thus action is an expectable feature of this time of life.

The task of integrating the new bodily self with older psychological imagoes or fantasies is carried out as well as most clearly manifest in the 'central masturbation fantasy'. This central theme, commonly manifest in relatively undisguised forms in the fantasies accompanying masturbation and masturbatory technique, reflects the evolving capacity of the youngster to integrate a physically mature body into his or her psychology and to integrate infantile sexuality into contemporary psychological life.

Melanie Klein Today: Developments in Theory and Practice. Volume I: Mainly Theory

Edited by Elizabeth Bott Spillius. London and New York: Routledge. 1988. Pp. 358.

Roger Shapiro

Roger Shapiro

Roger L. Shapiro
2312 Tracy Place NW, Washington, DC 20008

Roger Shapiro  

In this volume, Elizabeth Spillius has assembled papers written by members of the British Kleinian group in the period 1955 and 1985 and selected because they contain advances in theory. The papers allow the reader possibilities for considerations of comparative theory in that they are rich in clinical illustration. From the clinical material presented it becomes possible to think about alternative conceptual formulations to the ones the Kleinians put forward. This is important because the papers have a characteristic found in the work of members of most schools of psychoanalytic thought: the authors as a group develop their own language and build upon it, perhaps referring to antecedents in the work of Freud, but paying little or no attention to work of members of other schools of psychoanalysis and making scant effort to build bridges between their conceptualizations and the various concepts and terminologies that have evolved in other psychoanalytic groups. This includes, somewhat surprisingly, little or no reference to related work of other British object-relations theorists such as Winnicott and Fairbairn. This failure in analytic scholarship is a characteristic not only of the Kleinian group. It is as true of the American ego psychologists, the Kohut self psychologists, and the interpersonal psychoanalysts deriving from Sullivan. It has become the responsibility of the current generation of psychoanalysts to learn from attempts at comparison and integration of these bodies of theory.

Taken as a whole, the papers Spillius has collected are constructed on the foundation of Klein's conceptualization of the paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions, articulated by her in her 1946 paper, 'Notes on some schizoid mechanisms'. The papers demonstrate the centrality for the work of Klein's followers of the theory of the positions, two organizations of anxieties, defences and object relationships present concurrently in the adult mind. These are fundamental formulations in Kleinian theory, clarifying a variety of processes of progression and regression in ego functioning during development and in the clinical situation.

Other Times, Other Realities: Towards a Theory of Psychoanalytic Treatment

By Arnold H. Modell. Cambridge: Harvard University Press. 1990. Pp. 190.

Kenneth Newman

Kenneth Newman

Kenneth Newman
180 N. Michigan Avenue, Chicago, Il 60601

Kenneth Newman  

Arnold Modell has been writing at the frontiers of psychoanalytic theory and technique for more than two decades. His interests have extended to the more difficult clinical issues with special emphasis on the 'holding' environment, resistances to establishing a usable setting, and the schizoid defences, especially the withholding of affects. In his current book he brings all of his previous efforts together to attempt his most comprehensive examination of the role of analytic setting and relationship in the curative process.

Modell succeeds in addressing this complex subject, and the many questions and paradoxes it raises about reality and illusion, in a most stimulating fashion. He is not, however, simply concerned with posing philosophical dilemmas as they pertain to the major components of the analysis such as the setting, the therapeutic alliance, and the resistances. He is dedicated to re-examining and studying in depth the role of the setting as it facilitates the complex process of therapeutic action.

Panic: The Course of a Psychoanalysis

By Thorkil Vanggaard. New York and London: W. W. Norton. 1989. Pp. 144.

David Sachs

David Sachs

David M. Sachs
255 South 17th Street, Philadelphia, PA 19004

David Sachs  

There is a saying among politicians that timing is everything. An excellent candidate running for office at the wrong time may have no chance of winning; a poor candidate running at the right time may be elected easily. In contrast, the value of scientific contributions are thought to be independent of the climate of the times in which they are published. This is not the case in regard to Panic. Had it been published in the fifties or sixties Vanggaard's claim that his report is an example of a psychoanalysis would have been rejected out of hand, because the treatment was both insufficiently intense and far too brief in duration (three times per week for 51 sessions plus 10 sessions and a final telephone call 'session' scattered over an additional nine months).

Unfortunately, we live in a time when the 'quick fix' is the order of the day. Non-analytic therapists are willing to assert that their therapies are better because they can achieve results in far less time that are just as good as analysis. And they are quick to point out that faster means more 'cost efficient'. If psychoanalysts accept this approach to treatment, they place themselves in direct competition with behaviour modification, cognitive therapy, drug therapy, etc. On this battlefield psychoanalysis is bound to lose because it does not subscribe to the value system that claims that less is more, that faster is better and that difficult tasks can be accomplished easily. But it is precisely because psychoanalysis is under siege from these non-analytic quarters, that an analyst who asserts that he has found a way to complete an analysis in sixty-two sessions must be taken seriously.

The consequences of accepting Vanggaard's claim that his therapy is an analysis are staggering, but if they have merit psychoanalysts the world over will be obliged to come to grips with the changes this would make in our profession. For example, it would mean that the underpinnings of the standards of the International Psychoanalytical Association would have to be profoundly changed because these standards arise from a sincere belief that an analysis cannot be done as rapidly as Vanggaard asserts. If he is correct, his achievement would be a scientific contribution of great originality and significance. And in keeping with such importance, psychoanalysts have the right to expect him to exercise due diligence to show how his therapy achieves the goals his profession requires.

As an introduction to this complex and extremely broad theme I have to state that for the purpose of synthesis I shall have to leave out many fields of reflection. That is why in this paper I will only comment on some questions and above all delimit the theoretical framework of my speculations. As a matter of fact, both the concept of 'change' and the notion of 'psychic' obviously appear outside the field of psychoanalysis and even within it their possible shades of meaning can generate interminable discussions.

Quoting Freud when he introduces the theory of narcissism: 'But I am of opinion that that is just the difference between a speculative theory and a science erected on empirical interpretation. The latter will not envy speculation its privilege of having a smooth, logically unassailable foundation, but will gladly content itself with nebulous, scarcely imaginable basic concepts, which it hopes to apprehend more clearly in the course of its development, or which it is even prepared to replace by others' (1914, p. 77).

I shall first mention some texts that have particularly stimulated these reflections. Apart from Freud's classical works I refer to those of Argentine psychoanalysts: Angel Garma's (1974) about resistances during the final stages of psychoanalytical treatments; José Bleger's (1973) about the therapeutic aims in psychoanalysis; David Liberman's (1969) about the outcome of the therapeutic processes; Fernando Guiard's (1974) about the musical components of language at the end of treatment; M. & W. Baranger's & Jorge Mom's (1983) on process and non-process and Carlos M. Aslan's (1989) about common ground in psychoanalysis. The Capacity to Contain: Notes on its Function in Psychic Change

Roberto Speziale-Bagliacca

Roberto Speziale-Bagliacca

Roberto Speziale-Bagliacca
Via Bettolo 19
16031 Bogliasco
Genoa

Roberto Speziale-Bagliacca  

Solo recuerdo la emoción de las cosas,
y se me olvida todo lo demás.
Muchas son las lagunas de mi memoria.

[I remember only the emotion of things,
And I forget everything else.
Many are the gaps in my memory.]
(Antonio Machado)

Although it has been stated that the 'main work' of the analyst in promoting psychic change in the patient within the analytical process lies in his commitment to '"contain" the infantile aspects of the mind' (Meltzer, 1967), I believe that the great majority of psychoanalysts today would still agree with the thesis which united clinicians as diverse as Eissler and Rosenfeld in the Panel of the 20th IPA Congress, held in Paris in 1957, to the effect that the technique of psychoanalysis was 'one in which interpretation remains the exclusive or leading or prevailing tool' (Eissler, 1958).1

At that time there was certainly a great deal of discussion on the rule of abstinence, parameters and pseudo-parameters, but Rosenfeld (1987) could still maintain in his posthumously published work: 'the primary means by which the analyst achieves [his] aims is by precise verbal interpretation'. However, Rosenfeld himself, summarizing the views he held throughout his life, explains in other chapters how fundamental the preparatory work of containing is.

In my opinion, it may be misleading to regard the containing capacity of the analyst's 'mind' as a factor of transformation unless it is made clear that the container function and interpretation constitute a single system, so that, if either of these two factors is insufficient, psychic change will be compromised or incomplete (Speziale-Bagliacca, 1982), (1988).

The concept of containing in fact merges into that of the setting. As Grinberg (1981) points out, besides the external setting, which includes the better known formal aspects, there is the internal setting, which can rather be equated with the analytical attitude and also creates the therapist's 'style'. The internal setting, as the capacity to contain and the stage on which reverie is enacted, was forcefully presented to us in a new light by Bion (1962), (1963), who, dealing in particular with psychosis, had the courage to 'disturb our universe'.2

Edward Foulks

Edward Foulks

Department of Psychiatry Tulane Medical Center
1430 Tulane Avenue
New Orleans

Edward Foulks  

Two cases recently in psychoanalytic treatment illustrate the complex relations between oedipal structural and object-relational and narcissistic developmental issues. These cases raise questions regarding autonomy verses interdependency of libidinal and narcissistic developmental lines, and how interventions might be focused accordingly.

CASE 1: PRIMACY OF LIBIDINAL DEVELOPMENTAL FAILURES

The first case is a 26-year-old young man, who was so misused by both parents for their own needs that he became phobic about almost everything. His problems began when he was 5 years old and faced with going off to school. He had always been his mother's constant companion. He was not allowed out to play with other children because she feared that they would be too rough or he might catch an illness from them. At bedtime, his parents would sit by his bed until he fell asleep because he was terror stricken to be alone. Early every morning, he would creep into his parents' bedroom and crawl between them, a pattern which continued until he was 12 years old. He remembers feeling very anxious on Sunday in anticipation of Monday morning and having to separate from mother and get on the school bus. He remarked, 'on Monday morning, I would be in a real fit. I'd fake stomach pains. I'd do anything to stay home and it usually was successful'.

The patient's analysis began by his looking for reassurance and comforting words from me. He requested extended sessions, and was annoyed that he had to share my attention with other patients. I chose to orient my responses to libidinal developmental issues, and inquired whether he had harboured similar anxieties about his mother's attentions.

He responded by recalling that, as a child, he jealously guarded his mother so that she wouldn't spend much time with friends, even on the telephone. He'd become angry if she stopped to chat with someone on the street while they were walking. He wanted to be with her constantly, and feared that if he went away to school she would have someone in for a visit. He recalled an early memory in which he was walking with his mother, looking back at the living room and thinking that he saw someone sitting on the couch. He was sure of it, but his mother told him that there was no one there and that he should keep on moving. He began to fantasize that his mother would go back and love that person better than himself. I remarked that he seemed to fear the one intruding perhaps more than he feared losing nurturance, although the two were inextricably linked.

responseSet.title: 'To be the Best or not to Be, that is the Question …' on Enactment, Play and Acting out
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'To be the Best or not to Be, that is the Question …' on Enactment, Play and Acting out

Adeline Van Waning

Adeline Van Waning

Adeline Van Waning
Dentexstraat 5111
1017 XZ Amsterdam

Adeline Van Waning  

INTRODUCTION

When leafing through these pages, I feel as if I have just closed the door behind me after Harry has left the playroom. He has just made it clear to me, in words and deeds, that he thinks everything I say is rubbish. Yet, whatever these words of nonsense might have brought about, he is conspicuously relieved on leaving, after the free outpouring of his criticism: 'See you tomorrow!' …

Since this paper belongs to a series on the direct practice of psychoanalysis, as reflected in the daily analytic breath and pulse, you will be offered mainly 'undigested' raw material with verbatim reproduction of what analysand and analyst say, without extensive evaluations and theoretical reflections.

In order to structure the material to some extent, I made two basic choices: one concerning the content and one concerning the form in which the problems are expressed.

Regarding the content, the title of this paper indicates that we have to deal—in very general terms—with a narcissistic problem complex, the formation of conscience at the same time playing an important part in the development of this boy in the phase of latency. I shall concentrate on this aspect in my selection of material from Harry's analysis.

As to form, I have opted for a presentation in three parts, loosely connected with enacting, play and acting out respectively. Of course, these aspects cannot be sharply distinguished from each other in their occurrence, so that aspects of one form will also be observable in the examples of another. The aim of this form of presentation is merely to emphasize certain points.

Play and acting out are both forms of enactment, of 'expressing in deeds'. The examples of enactment given cover a period of a year and a half of the analysis, while the examples of play and acting out each extend over a limited period of a few weeks.

Before presenting the material, I shall tell you something about Harry and give an impressionistic working description of the terms enactment, play and acting out; the conclusion will include some discussion of the boundaries between them and the way these forms of action are handled in the analysis.

responseSet.title: Analysing the Matrix of Transference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analysing the Matrix of Transference

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

Thomas H. Ogden
1721 Scott Street
San Francisco
CA 94115

Thomas Ogden  

SUMMARY

In this paper, the background experiential states forming the matrix of transference are discussed in terms of the interplay of three modes of generating experience: the autistic-contiguous, the paranoid-schizoid and the depressive. Portions of three analyses are discussed in an effort to illustrate clinically some of the ways in which analytic technique is shaped by an understanding of the predominant mode or modes of experience forming the context of the transference-countertransference at any given moment. There is a focus on the ways in which the analyst's interventions must often be directed to the contextual level, or matrix, of transference (for example, the significance of the way the patient is thinking, talking or behaving) before it becomes possible to address other interrelated aspects of transference (for example, the unconscious symbolic meanings of what the patient is thinking, saying or enacting).

Variations in the Analytic Holding Environment

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower
15 West 75th Street
New York
NY 10023

Joyce Slochower  

SUMMARY

The concept of the holding environment has been used previously to characterize the treatment needs of severely regressed patients (Winnicott, 1963b). This holding of dependence is contrasted with the holding needs of narcissistic, apparently disengaged patients (Modell, 1976). The present paper considers the type of holding required by difficult, borderline patients. It is suggested that these borderline patients need a holding of their ruthlessness and rage, and also of analytic self blame. Clinical vignettes describe the holding of an anxiously demanding and of an overtly hateful borderline patient.

The patient in a holding environment evokes widely divergent countertransference reactions. These range from maternal nurturing feelings with extremely dependent patients (usually those emerging from profoundly schizoid states), to feelings of annihilation with narcissistic patients and of deep self doubt, anger and destructive wishes with demanding or hateful borderline patients. In the treatment of narcissistic and borderline patients, the holding environment metaphor may help the analyst tolerate these difficult countertransference feelings. Clinical examples of each type of countertransference reaction are presented.

responseSet.title: Psychic Change: The 'Becoming' of an Inquiry
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Change: The 'Becoming' of an Inquiry

Elizabeth De Bianchedi

Elizabeth De Bianchedi

Elizabeth T. De Bianchedi
Uriarte 2112
1425 Buenos Aires

Elizabeth De Bianchedi  

The objective of psychoanalysis is psychic change. It is a constant factor in our work and its realization can be found in practically all treatments, publications and training programmes for new psychoanalysts. It is also always included in the expansive field of the many different schools of psychoanalysis and of other disciplines partly derived from it.1 Its inclusion may be explicit, in the therapeutic goals and criteria of cure, or implicitly contained, in a task designed to promote either insight and working through, development and integration, the recovery or acquisition of psychic functions, etc. It has also been—and still is—included in the aims of masters, educators, shamans, Zen Buddhists and (in the past) alchemists and others, albeit employing rather different techniques to achieve it. Psychoanalysis seems to have more than venerable antecedents in the history of mankind, although a genius like Freud was necessary to discover it, name it and investigate its meaning—in other words, to put it into scientific and hence rationally communicable terms. After almost a century of Freud's first discoveries, these 'scientific', 'rationally communicable' terms have also undergone major developments and changes.

The word 'change' has a large number of synonyms and a variety of applications: transformation, evolution, mutation, metamorphosis, becoming, alteration, variation. It is contrasted with ideas of 'no-change', detention, immutability, permanence and invariability. These ideas have been discussed by philosophers since antiquity and constitute a problem of man as an individual and as a part of the species and of society. The concept of 'change' is also bound up with the questions: Is change always desirable? Any change? Or should we qualify change with some sign denoting change 'for the better'? If we do this, we are immediately faced with the questions 'for whom is it better?, 'who says it is better?' and 'what is better?' Better and worse, like good and bad, imply scales of values and belong to the field of ethics, with its different positions and controversies. Do we, as psychoanalysts, have anything to contribute to this field?

[[RunningHead]]

The Oedipus Complex Today: Clinical Implications

By Ronald Britton, Michael Feldman and Edna O'Shaughnessy. London: Karnac Books. 1989. Pp. 152.

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel
82 rue de l'Université, 75007 Paris

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel  

This little book, edited and with a preface by John Steiner, is presented as a collection of the three lectures delivered in September 1987 at the Melanie Klein Conference on the Oedipus complex. This Conference, held in London, was jointly organized by Joseph Sandler and Ruth Riesenberg Malcolm, the latter acting on behalf of the Melanie Klein Trust.

One is immediately intrigued by the title of this book of totally Kleinian (and secondarily Bionian) inspiration and with Melanie Klein's seminal (1945) article, 'The Oedipus complex in the light of early anxieties' figuring as Chapter I, which seems to suggest that it purports to express the views of the psychoanalytic community at large, or else that contemporary psychoanalysis is confined to the Kleinian school of thought. However, John Steiner writes, still in his preface: 'the three papers presented here seemed to reflect so well the current developments in modern Kleinian views of the Oedipus complex that the Trust decided to have the papers published'. A priori, this more restrictive phrase rings truer. Yet as the reader gets into the book and begins to reflect, he discovers that the title may be more pertinent than originally thought. In fact, due probably to reciprocal influences, a great many non-Kleinian analysts may well find that the views expressed here come close to their own, and whilst there may not be a consensus, certain of these will at least arouse their interest and a feeling of familiarity. This, I believe, will be true for quite a few French analysts.

The outstanding introduction written by Hanna Segal in her characteristically clear and precise style provides a historical outline of Melanie Klein's views concerning the Oedipus complex: an early Oedipus complex resulting from the relationship in which breast frustration (principally weaning) leads the infant to turn to its father and his penis. In her articles of 1928, 'Early stages of the Oedipus conflict', Melanie Klein considers that the Oedipus conflict comes into existence in a climate of hatred and sadism, but this initial view is gradually modified as she formulates the depressive position, leading her to the conclusion that the oedipal conflict originates in a climate of ambivalence, not hatred. Sadistic drives are brought into play, but accompanied by a desire to repair the damage caused to the parental couple. This change of direction is stated quite clearly in her 1945 article.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalyst in the Mirror: Doubts Galore but Few Certainties

Luciana Momigliano

Luciana Momigliano

Luciana Nissim Momigliano
20121 Milano
Via dei Chiostri 1

Luciana Momigliano  

SUMMARY

The clinical case of a female patient who seemed to be demanding from the analyst changes in her usual technique but in reality wanted her to modify her emotional attitude induced the author to confront the subject of anxiety about change felt to be 'catastrophic', considering it on this occasion from the point of view of the analyst. It is inevitable that the analyst too will initially resort to his or her old defences, succeeding only later by a great deal of hard work in transforming them and putting them in the service of the analysis itself.

The main subject of the paper is accompanied by a brief historical digression on the profound changes that have taken place in the years since Freud's death in the way the psychoanalytical movement as a whole thinks of and practises psychoanalysis, culminating in the 'pluralism' mentioned by Wallerstein, and by a personal example of the path travelled day after day by every analyst in his or her consulting room; the overall result is to hearten and encourage us about the future development of our work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Some Relationships of Fantasies of Perfection to the Calamities of Childhood

Arnold Rothstein

Arnold Rothstein

Arnold Rothstein
275 Central Park West
New York
NY 10024

Arnold Rothstein  

SUMMARY

In this paper narcissism conceived of as a fantasy of perfection is further redefined within an evolving structural model of psychic conflict. In pursuit of this goal a number of related propositions are explored.

1. A fantasy of perfection is best thought of as a compromise formation.

2. Fantasies of perfection have a development that is influenced, in part, by the development of the calamities of childhood.

3. Fantasies of perfection function as defences to diminish unpleasure associated with conflict. From this perspective a fantasy of perfection is a component of compromise formations.

4. The threat of the loss of the fantasy of perfection evokes anxiety while the sense the loss has occurred is associated with the experience of depressive affect.

5. Fantasies of perfection are important aspects of the compromise formations that constitute the superego. They are also important components of masochistic and narcissistic compromise formations characteristic of aspects of the ego. These compromise formations are intimately related to defensive identificatory relationships between the ego and superego.

Analytic data are presented to demonstrate the value of these theoretical considerations.

[[RunningHead]]

Fifteen Clinical Accounts of Psychoanalysis—A Further Invitation

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

David Tuckett  

This part of the Journal is devoted to fifteen papers arising from our wish to facilitate a greater degree of shared understanding of diversity and common ground in current psychoanalysis and the invitation we made to psychoanalysts to submit to us examples of the way they currently work (IJPA, 71:1). That invitation arose out of Dr Robert Wallerstein's address to the 36th International Psychoanalytical Congress in Rome and the discussion which it provoked. We requested those submitting papers to try 'to describe a period of work with a patient and the relevant background. Some detail of what patient and analyst actually said to each other should be included and what the analyst thought or felt at the time would be relevant. Theoretical discussion should be separated from the clinical account as much as possible … Discussion should try to show what the particular analyst means by analysing the transference or resistance, waiting for associations, making reconstructions etc … only case reports about patients seen in full psychoanalytical treatment, as defined within the psychoanalyst's local society, are eligible' (IJPA, 71:2).

In the event some 26 authors responded to the invitation made both in the Journal and by additional letters and promptings by the Joint Editors and the Regional Editors. The papers selected for publication are those that best fulfilled the requirement that the clinical account clearly describe a period of work in such a way that the reader could understand what the patient and psychoanalyst actually said to each other and what the psychoanalyst's reasons for his interventions were. We have included two cases where the patient was a child. Beyond these very broad criteria we have tried to be as unselective as possible and we offer these accounts as examples of the way psychoanalysis is being practised with no implication as to how we feel it should be.

The end result is fifteen papers. Three are from North America; three from South America and nine from Europe. Of the European papers all but one were submitted in the analyst's local language and translated into English by the Journal. Details of the fifteen authors' training and orientation are given below along with information about the frequency of sessions. Three of the analyses were conducted at five times a week and twelve at four times a week. About half the authors were Training Analysts and their self-described orientations include those that could be classified as fairly strictly 'Freudian' (2); 'Freudian plus' (2); eclectic-independent-non-denominational (5); 'Kleinian' (2); 'Kleinian plus' (3); and 'Freudian-Post-Kleinian' (1). It will be for readers to determine how far these orientations do or do not get reflected in the details of technical practice. All the authors have, of course, taken considerable steps to protect the confidentiality of their patients' material.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy

Merton Gill M.D.

Merton Gill

Merton M. Gill, M.D.
The University of Illinois at Chicago Department of Psychiatry (M/C 913)
912 South Wood Street
Chicago, Illinois 60612

Merton Gill  

Sir:

Wallerstein's (1989) assessment in this Journal of my views on the relationship between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy, however well intentioned, is a serious misrepresentation. In my 1984 paper in which I stated my revised views, I anticipated such misunderstanding and attempted to prevent it. If Wallerstein's paper is a criterion, that effort has failed.

This becomes clearest when Wallerstein compares my view with Alexander's. There is an important similarity and an important difference between my position and Alexander's, but Wallerstein does not present either correctly.

The similarity is one which is not often referred to in discussing Alexander. It is that he took a two-person view of the analytic situation, but he did not realize how important a difference this is from the classical analytic position, nor did he systematically follow through on this new position. He wrote in 1956: 'In the actual interpersonal—patient–therapist—equation the therapist does not figure as a constant factor, a pure intellect or a blank screen, but as a distinct individual. The contrast between the transference experience and the past in each case is also determined by the analyst's countertransference and his total personality' (p. 280). But in the same place he also wrote: 'no radically new principles have been proposed applying either to the theory or the technique of psychoanalysis' (p. 279).

So the similarity is that Alexander took a two-body (person) view of the analytic situation, a view I have espoused for some time. The difference between Alexander and myself is that Alexander not only did not advocate the analysis of this interpersonal interaction but rather, he explicitly proposed manipulating the interaction by employing 'planfully selected interpersonal climates' (1956, p. 281) as a central therapeutic technique.

I, on the contrary, am describing a therapy which, although it does not conform to the usual extrinsic analytic criteria of frequency and position, employs the central technique of psychoanalysis, namely, the analysis of suggestion, that is, the analysis of the interaction. Indeed, the technique I espouse seeks to do so even more systematically and thoroughly than is often the case in the practice of psychoanalysis. In short, in that respect what I propose is the antithesis of Alexander's proposal.

Harold Blum  

PSYCHIC CHANGE: DEVELOPMENTS IN THE THEORY OF PSYCHOANALYTIC TECHNIQUE was chosen as the overarching theme for the 37th IPA Congress, Buenos Aires, 29 July-2 August 1991. This theme addresses both clinical and theoretical issues of fundamental importance. The goals of psychoanalysis have been described in many ways, e.g. insight, conflict resolution, and from different clinical and theoretical perspectives, e.g. analysis of a transference neurosis and genetic reconstruction. All the goals of clinical analysis converge in psychic change whether conceptualized in restoring the ego to optimal function, structural change, or personality reorganization, etc. How is psychic change effected in analytic work, why and when does change occur, and what are the technical principles and specific technical agents of change? How has analysis changed or developed in recent years, both within the field and for the particular analyst? Further considerations lead to the theory of technique. What are the underlying assumptions and models that guide the analyst's listening, observing, and organizing the analytic data? No analyst can approach the analytic situation without some theoretical notions and convictions, while, at the same time, every analysis is a potentially new learning experience for the analyst as well as the patient.

The goals of analysis are defined differently in different analytic frameworks and are not always made explicit. The patient may have the immediate goal of symptom relief or magical cure from the modern Delphic Oracle, but the analyst's formulations of desired psychic change may be very different. There are differences in the goal of analytic change that have evolved long after Freud's own renowned metaphors of 'making the unconscious conscious', and 'where id was there shall ego be'.

We are interested in the structure of the psychic changes achieved, i.e. structural change, and whether psychic change is enduring and resistant to regression. Both process and outcome changes have been observed, e.g. in the capacity to free associate and in greater ego flexibility with freedom from work and play inhibitions. The pathways from insight to working through and adaptive change, the relatively silent ego processes of integration and reorganization, have all been subject to new analytic consideration.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy

Merton Gill M.D.

Merton Gill

Merton Gill  

Sir:

I am grateful to Wallerstein for his thoughtful and detailed reply to my criticism. I believe it is much clearer now where we agree and where we disagree.

I am glad that he accepts the centrality of the interpersonal aspect of the psychoanalytic situation and that he considers that an insistence on this point might be more important than he had at first thought.

He has clarified that his view of my relationship to Alexander has a number of facets, with some of which he agrees and others he disagrees. He sees that my understanding of the 'corrective emotional experience' (Wallerstein, 1990) does indeed differ from Alexander's in that I do not advocate the manipulation of the relationship which Alexander proposed. He still feels however that I am like Alexander in what he considers to be a blurring of the relationship between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy.

It is here that we disagree. My opinion is that I am sharpening the distinction and that it is he (and Rangell) who is blurring it. He says it is sometimes difficult to tell whether a therapy is psychotherapy or psychoanalysis. I believe it is always possible to tell. If the therapist is attempting to deal with the analysis of the transference as completely and thoroughly as he can, the therapy is analysis. If he is not it is psychotherapy. That it may be hard to tell from an isolated segment of the therapy what his intent is I agree, but the therapy as a whole should tell the story. Of course there is a matter of definition here. He might say that if it is not possible to analyze the transference thoroughly the treatment is a psychotherapy. I prefer a definition in terms of aim rather than achievement. I have argued this view in a recent concluding chapter to Oremland's monograph entitled Interpretation and Interaction: Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy (in press).

As another matter of definition I have suggested that if psychoanalytic technique, by which I do indeed mean the centrality of the analysis of the transference, though not exclusively, is the goal, the treatment is psychoanalytic. It is to be distinguished from psychoanalysis proper. I am having trouble finding a good name for the former but in the chapter to which I referred I called it 'psychoanalytic therapy' resurrecting thereby a term used by Alexander & French, but I hope not thus expressing an agreement with them.

Body Image, Sexuality and the Psychotic Core

M. Laufer

M. Laufer

M. Eglé Laufer
48 Abbey Gardens
London NW8 9AT

M. Laufer  

SUMMARY

The mental representation of the body—that is, the body image—is a basic element of the person's relationship to himself as well as to reality. From a developmental point of view the body image only becomes finally integrated to form the basis of the person's sexual and gender identity at the end of adolescence. This final body image can contain distortions compared to the person's real body (as viewed by others) and thus can represent a break with some aspects of external reality.

In this paper I use material from the analyses

70

of two patients, one adult and one adolescent, to show the compelling quality of the need to maintain a specific body image, and how it becomes related to their sexuality. I also discuss how, when the body image is one that is distorted, it forms a psychotic core to the personality in that it must affect the person's relationship to external reality.

[[RunningHead]]

Schreber: Father and Son

By Han Israëls. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1989. Pp. 376.

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

Ralph E. Roughton
1175 Peachtree Street, Atlanta, Georgia 30361.

Ralph Roughton  

Han Israëls is a Dutch sociologist whose research interest is the history of psychoanalysis. This book, a translation of his 1980 Ph.D. thesis Schreber, Vader en Zoon, is important on three counts: (1) it clarifies certain of the biographical facts about Paul Schreber and his father; (2) it points to methodological errors and to alleged political misuse of the Schreber material by later psychoanalytic writers; and (3) although this is not one of the author's primary aims, it does raise again, in passing, the vexing question of the relative importance of early trauma v. intrapsychic conflict in the causality of mental illness.

Paul Schreber's Memoirs of My Nervous Illness is indeed a classic document in the history of psychoanalysis, and the controversies surrounding it make an excellent choice of study for a sociologist interested in that history. Israëls is a meticulous and tireless researcher (he persisted for three years until he obtained access to some archival material in East Germany); and in delving systematically into the lives of the two Schrebers, he has produced an authoritative biography backed by the thorough research that has been needed.

Beginning with Freud's use of the Memoirs in his discourse on the mechanisms of paranoia (1911), 'the Schreber case' has fascinated psychoanalytic readers and has attracted numerous authors who have used the Memoirs to 'prove' widely divergent points of view. In addition to psychoanalytic and psychiatric writers, the work has attracted critics and polemicists from the fields of feminism, structuralism, Marxism, and radical theology. However, Israëls confines his study to the psychoanalytic and psychiatric literature and his theorizing to the father-son relationship.

The underlying, but larger, subject in Israëls' book that should be taken seriously by those interested in the future of psychoanalysis is his critique of the unscientific nature and the methodological flaws with which psychoanalytic theory has been historically constructed. Charging that 'theory is formulated so that it will always be possible to find evidence to fit it, but also so that it is difficult to imagine how the theory might be refuted' (p. xx), Israëls attempts to prune away errors of fact, to dispute conclusions based on faulty data, and to question the motives of some of the Schreber scholars as being more personal or political than scientific.

[[RunningHead]]

Comments on the Treatment of a Narcissistic Patient

Joan Coderch

Joan Coderch

Joan Coderch
Balmes 317 – 102ay10B
08006 Barcelona

Joan Coderch  

In this work I propose to illustrate some aspects of my way of understanding and conducting the psychoanalytic process and also my way of interpreting resistance and transference. In order to do this I will present clinical material describing my treatment of a patient with a markedly narcissistic personality. At the same time, I will also discuss the relationship observed in this patient between narcissism and the psychotic side of the personality, as understood in the sense described by Bion when he divides the personality into its neurotic and psychotic aspects. I will attempt to show that psychotic functioning is a tactic used by the narcissistic self to deny reality—i.e. the existence of and need for an object and the self's dependency on that object—in order to be able to sustain its omnipotent role.

In the course of my first interviews with the patient, a young bachelor I will refer to as Mr A, he told me that, albeit unwillingly, he had felt different from other children since his early adolescence. He said he felt unhappy and indifferent and did not really want to live. He explained that he also felt very worried by his tendency to turn to pornographic magazines and videos in order to achieve sexual arousal. He also told me he had neither the ability nor the drive to get ahead in the world of work as he would have liked. At that time, he was doing a job that was far beneath his true professional training but did not know how to achieve a more satisfactory level. He also said that he had been smoking a lot of cigarettes every day for years and as a result suffered from throat problems and irritations. He often tormented himself with the idea that he would get cancer as a result of this habit but despite this he still could not seriously think of giving up.

Mr A displayed anxiety when he spoke to me of his parents. Their relationship, he said, had been cold and distant. This was especially true of his father, whom he criticized for working too hard despite his advanced age. He spoke of his mother as nosey and meddlesome, even though he said she had always looked after him very well.

[[RunningHead]]

Dimensions of Psychoanalysis

Edited by Joseph Sandler. London: Karnac Books. 1989. Pp. 263.

Helmut Junker

Helmut Junker

Helmut Junker
Lerchenfeldstr. 24, D3500 Kassel

Helmut Junker  

This volume comprises essays which were originally given as inaugural lectures for The Freud Memorial Chair at the University College London between 1975 and 1988. As James Lighthill put it with some pride: 'This work celebrates 14 years of the Freud Memorial Professorship programme' (p. xv).

Thus the stage is set: memory and celebration. Inevitably, each author has to look back and to combine his personally tinted thoughts of the day with the traditional language of the field. As 'the preparation of an inaugural lecture is something that is never taken lightly' (Sandler, p. 1), all contributors strove to give their best by synthesizing knowledge with personal views and by not expressing opposing ideas and approaching controversial topics. Freud is the central character and therefore no one will be surprised that all the papers, scrutinized as they are, are wrapped in and imbued by respect and eulogy. When celebrating, one is usually in a state of elevation in which one tends to centre on established ideas; this is very helpful for those who learn. However, the historical Freud sometimes gets distorted, for example Schafer misunderstands him as a man abundant with humanistic ambitions (pp. 13f.)

The reviewer, with German as his native language and coming from academic and psychoanalytic circles, appreciates the stringency of thought and the smooth presentation, less known in his professional surroundings. Sometimes the adjectives sound like epitheta ornantia; they calm the reader, provide clarity and delay questions. The battlefield of thoughts must be found elsewhere, not in these eleven articles.

Each contribution aims for plausibility rather than proof. This facilitates reading and brings about a clear, although not oversimplified, distinction between current theories. The languages of psychoanalysis, however, are largely spread over the history of the discipline, so readers will be confronted with vocabulary which can only claim coherence and meaning according to the individual theory. The process of understanding on the part of the reader depends on whether the connotations of the different terms are already known; the dictionary definition alone would not be helpful.
